Indicator.Remediation: Review the list and ensure that there are no unnecessary principals who can set resource-based constrained delegation on krbtgt.

Indicator.Name: Non-standard schema permissions
Indicator.Description: This indicator looks for additional principals with any permissions beyond generic Read to the schema partitions.Schema is one of three main Active Directory naming context. It contains every object attribute definitions of the forest.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
By default, modification permissions on schema are limited to Schema Admins. These permissions grant the trusted Principal complete control over Active Directory.

References

Dangerous ACLs expose schema objects (attack path) | vuln_permissions_schema | ANSSI
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} non default ACEs on the schema.
Indicator.Remediation: It is recommended to revert schema permissions to default state. See indicator description for additional info and link.

Indicator.Name: Changes to AD Display Specifiers in the past 90 days
Indicator.Description: This indicator looks for changes made in the past 90 days to the adminContextMenu attribute on AD display specifiers. This attribute controls the right-click menus presented to users in the domain using MMC tools such as AD Users and Computers. Modifying these attributes can potentially allow attackers to get users to run arbitrary code if those menu options are clicked.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Attackers may utilize context menus as a stealthy way of getting various users in a domain to execute code. Modifying this attribute requires special permissions granted by default only to Domain Admins and Enterprise Admins and also requires the user to click on the illicit context menu item. See the this blog post for additional information.

References

Semperis blog | Active Directory Security: Abusing Display Specifiers
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} changes to Admin Context Menu on Display Specifier in the last {1} days.
Indicator.Remediation: Review the changes and ensure they are legitimate. It is also recommended to revert write permissions on Display Specifiers to default state (only privileged users have write permissions).

Indicator.Name: Non-privileged users with access to gMSA passwords
Indicator.Description: This indicator looks for principals listed within MSDS-groupMSAmembership that are not in the built-in admin groups.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
An attacker that controls access to the gMSA account can retrieve passwords for resources managed with gMSA.

References

Dangerous ACLs expose gMSA key objects (attack path) | ANSSI
Indicator.ResultMessage: There are {0} Group Managed Service Accounts that unprivileged principals can potentially read their password
Indicator.Remediation: Ensure that there are no unnecessary principals who can read Group Managed Service Account passwords via the msDS-GroupMSAMembership attribute.

Indicator.Name: gMSA not in use
Indicator.Description: This indicator checks if there are enabled group Managed Service Account (gMSA) objects in the domain.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
The group Managed Service Account (gMSA) feature in Windows Server 2016 allows automatic rotation of passwords for service accounts, making them much more difficult for attackers to compromise. The feature should be used whenever possible for service accounts.

References

Get started with Group Managed Service Accounts
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} domains with no gMSA objects enabled.
Indicator.Remediation: Group Managed Service Accounts should be used to protect service accounts. See description for more information.

Indicator.Name: Unsecured DNS configuration
Indicator.Description: This indicator looks for DNS zones configured with ZONE_UPDATE_UNSECURE, which allows updating a DNS record anonymously.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise: An attacker could leverage this exposure to arbitrarily add a new DNS record or replace an existing record to spoof a management interface, then wait for incoming connections in order to steal credentials.
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} DNS zones configured with ZONE_UPDATE_UNSECURE.
Indicator.Remediation:
Reconfigure DNS zones to only allow secure updates using the following command: dnscmd /Config /AllowUpdate 2

References

Misconfigured DNS zones | ANSSI

Indicator.Name: Weak certificate cipher
Indicator.Description: This indicator looks for certificates stored in active directory with keysize smaller than 2048 bits or utilize DSA encryption.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Weak certificates can be abused by attackers to gain access to systems who use certificate authentication.

References

Weak or vulnerable certificates | ANSSI
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} certificates with weak configuration.
Indicator.Remediation: Problematic certificates need to be revoked and re-issued. Children certificates must also be re-issued. Expired certificates should also be purged from trusted certificates stores. When issuing certificates, the following requirements should be met: Use RSA or ECDSA for certificate signature (don't use DSA), RSA key length is equal to or greater than 2048 and an up-to-date library is used to generate the RSA key.

Indicator.Name: Domain trust to a third-party domain without quarantine
Indicator.Description: This indicator looks for outbound forest trusts that has Quarantine flag set to false, which means that the trusted domain is not subject to SID filtering.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
An attacker having compromised the remote domain can spoof any user or machine on the local domain (except for accounts with a RID lower than 1000, excluding built-in accounts and groups). This attacker can therefore access every resource on the local domain. If a dangerous control path is exposed to any "spoofable" account (virtually any account other than the built-in ones), the attacker could also escalate his privileges up to "Domain Admins" and compromise the entire forest.

References

Unfiltered outbound domain trust relationship | ANSSI
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} outbound trusts where Quarantine is disabled or SID history is enabled.
Indicator.Remediation:
Unless domain migration is currently ongoing, use the following command to enable Quarantine for external trusts: NETDOM TRUST /domain: /Quarantine yes

And use the following command to disable SID history for forest trusts: NETDOM TRUST /domain: /EnableSIDHistory:No

Indicator.Name: Outbound forest trust with SID History enabled
Indicator.Description: This indicator checks for outbound forest trusts that have the TRUST_ATTRIBUTE_TREAT_AS_EXTERNAL flag set to true.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
If TRUST_ATTRIBUTE_TREAT_AS_EXTERNAL is set to true, the security of a cross-forest trust is relaxed and trusts are treated as equivalent to external trusts.

An attacker that compromises the remote Active Directory forest, with this set to true, can spoof most users and computers in the local Active Directory forest. This would allow the attacker to act as local users or computers and access resources they have privileges in. An attacker can also use this for privilege elevation and compromise the local forest.

References

Active Directory Security Best Practices | Semperis

Outbound forest trust relationships with sID History enabled | ANSSI
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} outbound trusts where SIDHistory is enabled.
Indicator.Remediation:
Unless a forest migration is currently in process, SID filtering should be enabled on the trust.

Organizations should understand the risk of leaving this enabled for an extended period of time.

The following command can be run to enable: NETDOM TRUST /domain: /EnableSIDHistory no

Indicator.Name: Users with permissions to set Server Trust Account
Indicator.Description: Checks for permissions on the domain NC head that enables a user to set a UAC flag - Server_Trust_Account on computer objects. This flag gives that computer object special permissions similar to a domain controller.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise: A persistence technique where an attacker with DS-Install-Replica permissions can manipulate the userAccountControl attribute to promote a compromised computer account to Domain Controller status. This enables privilege escalation through domain replication privileges, allowing attacks like DCSync to extract credentials.
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} objects with install replica permissions.
Indicator.Remediation: Ensure that there are no unnecessary install replica permissions and investigate suspicious permissions.

Indicator.Name: Dangerous control paths expose certificate templates
Indicator.Description: This indicator looks for non-default principals with the ability to write properties on a certificate template.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Controlling certificate templates allows one to have the certificate authority issue an arbitrary certificate. It becomes possible to obtain a smartcard authentication certificate for any user, thus stealing his identity.

References

NIST-SP1800-16B

Dangerous ACLs expose certificate template objects (attack path) | ANSSI
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} certificate templates on which unprivileged users can write properties.
Indicator.Remediation: Unprivileged users should not be able to write properties on certificate templates. Doing so potentially gives them the ability to escalate their access and create vulnerable certificates to enroll. Remove unnecessary permissions from the certificate template.

Indicator.Name: Dangerous control paths expose certificate containers
Indicator.Description: This indicator looks for non-default principals with permissions on the NTAuthCertificates container. This container holds the intermediate CA certificates that can be used to authenticate to AD.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
These control paths allow adding a malicious certificate authority, which allow an attacker to authenticate as arbitrary users or services.

References

Dangerous ACLs expose certificate containers (attack path) | ANSSI
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} principals that have non default permissions on the NTAuthCertificates object.
Indicator.Remediation: Unprivileged users should not have permissions on the NTAuthCertificates. Doing so potentially gives them the ability to escalate their access and make the domain trust a rouge CA. Remove unnecessary permissions from the object.

Indicator.Name: Non-default access to DPAPI key
Indicator.Description: This indicator uses API calls to check whether each DC has non-default principals permitted to retrieve the domain DPAPI backup key (using LsaRetrievePrivateData).
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
An attacker could recover all domain data encrypted via DPAPI, if they gain access to such data.

References

Dangerous ACLs expose DPAPI key objects (attack path) | ANSSI
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} DCs that allows non default principals to read private data from the LSA policy database.
Indicator.Remediation: These permissions allow a principal to use LsaRetrievePrivateData to retrieve the domain DPAPI backup keys. Review these principals, investigate and remove any unneeded permissions using adsiedit.msc or the ldp utility.

Indicator.Name: Report suspicious MFA activity disabled
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks if the setting to report suspicious MFA activity is enabled.

Required permissions

  • Policy.Read.All
  • GroupMember.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Social engineering attacks such as phishing or MFA fatigue attacks can be used to trick a user into authorizing an MFA request they did not initiate. While systems like number matching reduce the ability for these attacks to work, in some instances the attacker may have gained access to the user's password.

When report suspicious activity is enabled, users can report suspicious MFA requests in interactive MFA scenarios with push notifications or phone calls. This allows the user to be proactively involved in securing their account.

References

Configure Microsoft Entra multifactor authentication settings | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Report suspicious activity is set to {0} for Group {1}.
Indicator.Remediation:
It is recommended to have the "Report suspicious activity" setting enabled for all users. Please note that if you are using the "Microsoft managed" option, the "Report suspicious activity" setting will be disabled.

To configure the "Report suspicious activity" setting, open the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link), browse to Protection > Authentication methods > Settings > Report suspicious activity, and confirm that State is set to Enabled and Target is All users.

Indicator.Name: AD Certificate Authority with Web Enrollment - ESC8
Indicator.Description:
This indicator attempts to identify AD CS servers in the domain that accept NTLM authentication to Web Enrollment Services. The script enumerates CAs in the Enrollment Services container, resolves their IP and attempts NTLM authentication to https://IP/certsrv and http://IP/certsrv. Note: This indicator currently does not identify EPA or other mitigations suggested by Microsoft.

The indicator will return a Passed if an enrollment service is contacted, but NTLM authentication is denied (positive effect on the posture score) for any endpoint.

The indicator will return an IoE Found if NTLM authentication is available on an enrollment service (negative effect on security posture) on any endpoint.

The indicator will Fail to Run (no effect on security posture score) if one of the following is true for all endpoints:

  • Cannot Resolve - Enrollment Service Certificate found in AD CS container, but address cannot be resolved
  • Unreachable - IP is resolved, but service cannot be reached
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Attackers can exploit misconfigured Active Directory Certificate Services (AD CS) Web Enrollment to launch NTLM and Kerberos relay attacks, enabling them to authenticate as privileged users or computer accounts. One notable instance occurred in July 2021, when attackers combined this method with the PetitPotam attack on the MS-EFSRPC protocol, using only network access to coerce authentication. Other techniques allow any domain user to coerce a domain controller or computer account's authentication, relay it to AD CS Web Enrollment, and request a certificate for privileged access. This can ultimately result in domain takeover. For more information on this attack, often referred to as ESC8, consult detailed write-ups available online.

References

KB5005413: Mitigating NTLM Relay Attacks on Active Directory Certificate Services (AD CS)
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} AD CS Enrollment endpoints. A 'True' result in the table below means that NTLM authentication to the endpoint was successful. A 'False' result means that the script was unable to authenticate - this means that a mitigation MAY be in place. An 'Unable to resolve' result means that the script was unable to resolve an IP address to the endpoint.
Indicator.Remediation: As per Microsoft guidance, ensure EPA is enabled and NTLM authentication is disabled on all AD CS servers.

Indicator.Name: Privileged Users with Weak Password Policy
Indicator.Description: This indicator looks for privileged users in each domain that don't have a strong password policy enforced, according to ANSSI framework. It checks both FGPP (Fine-Grained Password Policy) and the password policy applied to the domain. A strong password as defined by ANSSI is at least 8 characters long and updated no later than every 3 years.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Weak passwords are easier to crack via brute-force attacks, they can provide attackers opportunities for moving laterally or escalating privileges. The risk is even higher for privileged accounts, for when easily compromised, they improve the attacker's chance to quickly advance within the network.

References

NIST.SP.800-63-3

Privileged group members with weak password policy | ANSSI
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} privileged users with a password policy but whose compliance with them could not be determined
Indicator.Remediation: Apply appropriate password policies for privileged users.

Indicator.Name: NTFRS SYSVOL Replication
Indicator.Description: This indicator looks for indication of usage of FRS for sysvol replication. Domain Controllers are configured to use the NTFRS replication protocol (especially for SYSVOL replication). This protocol is obsolete and unnecessarily adds administrative interfaces to domain controllers. In addition, this protocol is no longer supported by the latest versions of Windows Server, which prevents migration to the latest versions.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
NTFRS is an older protocol that has been replaced by DFSR. Attackers that can manipulate NTFRS vulnerabilities to compromise SYSVOL can potentially change GPOs and logon scripts to propagate malware and move laterally across the environment.

References

Migrate SYSVOL replication to DFS Replication | Microsoft Learn

SYSVOL replication through NTFRS | ANSSI
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} domains that are suspected to use NTFRS for SYSVOL replication.
Indicator.Remediation: This protocol is obsolete since Windows Server 2008.

Indicator.Name: Abnormal Password Refresh
Indicator.Description: This indicator looks for user accounts with a recent pwdLastSet change without a corresponding password replication.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise: If an administrator marks the option "User must change password at next logon" and then clears (i.e. unchecks) the option later, the pwdLastSet is updated without the password actually being changed. This could be an administrative error or an attempt to bypass the organization's password policy.
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} user(s) with a mismatch between pwdLastSet and unicodepwd. This could indicate an attempt to bypass the organization's password policy.
Indicator.Remediation: Ensure that users change their password at least once every 6 months.

Indicator.Name: Accounts with Constrained Delegation configured to krbtgt
Indicator.Description: This indicator checks for accounts that have constrained delegation configured to the KRBTGT account.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
In Active Directory it is possible to create a Kerberos delegation to the KRBTGT account. This type of delegation to a user or computer would allow that principal to generate a ticket granting service (TGS) request to the KRBTGT account as any user.

An attacker may attempt to compromise any account that has a delegation to the KRBTGT account. This particular type of configuration allows for an attack similar to a Golden Ticket attack.

References

Constrained authentication delegation to a domain controller service | ANSSI
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} account(s) with Constrained Delegation configured to the krbtgt service.
Indicator.Remediation:
It is recommended to not allow constrained delegation to the KRBTGT account.

This type of delegation to a user or computer would allow that principal to generate a ticket granting service (TGS) request to the KRBTGT account as any user.

An attacker may attempt to compromise any account that has a delegation to the KRBTGT account. This particular type of configuration allows for an attack similar to a Golden Ticket attack.

The delegations identified should be removed.

Indicator.Name: Accounts with Constrained Delegation configured to ghost SPN
Indicator.Description: When computers are decommissioned, delegation configuration to them is often not cleaned up. Such a delegation could allow an attacker that has the privileges to write to the ServicePrincipalName attribute of another service account, to escalate privileges on those services. This could result in escalating privileges by moving laterally across the infrastructure. This indicator looks for accounts that have Constrained Delegation configured to ghost SPNs.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
This type of attack should be easy to spot as the configured SPN within the msds-allowedtodelegateto attribute will not exist on the domain. However, if they are found, they would represent a significant risk and should be mitigated quickly.

References

Constrained authentication delegation to a domain controller service | ANSSI
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} account(s) with Constrained Delegation configured to ghost SPN(s).
Indicator.Remediation: Remove any Kerberos delegations to ghost SPN(s).

Indicator.Name: SYSVOL Executable Changes
Indicator.Description: This indicator looks for modifications to executable files within SYSVOL. It only examines files and executables that have read access to them.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise: Changes to the executable files within SYSVOL should be accounted for by the administrators. If the change can not be accounted for, investigate the change looking for potential weakening of security posture and why the change was made.
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} SYSVOL executables that have been recently modified.
Indicator.Remediation: Ensure all recently created/modified executables are safe.

Indicator.Name: Accounts with altSecurityIdentities configured
Indicator.Description: It is possible to add values to the altSecurityIdentities attribute and essentially impersonate that account. The altSecurityIdentities attribute is a multi-valued attribute used to create mappings for X.509 certificates and external Kerberos accounts. This indicator checks for accounts with the altSecurityIdentities attribute configured.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
This type of attack may be easy to spot as it is rarely configured during normal operations. However, it is possible for this attribute to be configured genuinely.

References

Constrained authentication delegation to a domain controller service | ANSSI
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} account(s) with altSecurityIdentities configured.
Indicator.Remediation: Remove any entries in altSecurityIdentities attributes that are not explicitly required.

Indicator.Name: FGPP not applied to Global group
Indicator.Description: This indicator looks for FGPP targeted to a Universal or Domain Local group.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Changing a group's scope settings from Global group to Universal or Domain Local group, will result in FGPP settings no longer applying to that group, and decreasing its password security controls.

References

NIST.SP.800-63-3
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} Group(s) with no FGPP applied to them.
Indicator.Remediation: Confirm that the following groups should be Universal or Domain Local, otherwise change their scope settings to Global for FGPP to apply to them.

Indicator.Name: Changes to Pre-Windows 2000 Compatible Access Group membership
Indicator.Description: This indicator looks for changes to the built-in group "Pre-Windows 2000 Compatible Access". This group grants read-only access to Active Directory.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
As part of a layered approach to security and to ensure that non-authenticated users cannot read Active Directory, it's best to ensure this group does not contain the "Anonymous Logon" or "Everyone" groups.

References

Understanding the Risks of Pre-Windows 2000 Compatible Access Settings | Semperis blog
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} changes to Pre-Windows 2000 Compatible Access group.
Indicator.Remediation: Confirm that any addition or removals from Pre-Windows 2000 Compatible Access group are valid and properly accounted for.

Indicator.Name: Non-synced Entra user that is eligible for a privileged role
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks for Entra users that are eligible for a high-privilege role and have the proxyAddress attribute, but are not synchronized with an AD account.

Required permissions:

  • User.Read.All
  • RoleManagement.Read.Directory
  • Directory.Read.All
  • Organization.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
An attacker can abuse SMTP matching to synchronize a user they have compromised in Active Directory to a user in Entra ID that is eligible for a high-privilege role. This process overwrites the user password in Entra ID and can result in privilege escalation over the Entra tenant.

References

SMTP Matching Abuse in Entra ID | Semperis blog
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} user(s) eligible for high privilege roles that are not synchronized with AD.
Indicator.Remediation:
Ensure that the users indicated in the results are configured for MFA, which will prevent the ability for an attacker to authenticate as the compromised user in Entra ID, even if the attacker attempts SMTP matching abuse.

Further, organizations can disable soft-matching in their Entra tenant to protect against this takeover. Disabling soft-matching should not be a replacement for enablement of MFA on privileged users in Entra ID.

Full details on the attack, detection and preventative remediation can be found in the referenced Semperis article.

Indicator.Name: Inheritance enabled on AdminSDHolder object
Indicator.Description: This indicator checks for inheritance enabled on the access control list (ACL) of the AdminSDHolder object.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
The AdminSDHolder object in Active Directory holds the permissions that will be applied to privileged groups and users, including Domain Admins. By default, permissions on privileged users are more restrictive to protect them from compromise. If an organization adjusts the permissions on the AdminSDHolder object, including enabling inheritance, they may weaken the security of these privileged users.

An attacker may discover these weaker permissions on privileged users and groups, and with a greater surface area, an attacker has a stronger chance of formulating attack paths to compromise users such as a Domain Admin, and then compromising Active Directory.

References

AdminSDHolder to improve Active Directory Security | Semperis

7 Active Directory Misconfigurations to Find and Fix-Now - Semperis

Appendix C - Protected Accounts and Groups in Active Directory | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} domains containing an AdminSDHolder container with inheritance enabled.
Indicator.Remediation:
In addition to disabling inheritance on the AdminSDHolder object, organizations should investigate the ACL on the object to ensure it aligns with their security policy. Use attack path analysis software, such as Forest Druid, to analyze attack paths to privileged users in Active Directory.

Organizations, if changing the permissions on AdminSDHolder, should have an established process in place for awareness.

Unplanned changes to AdminSDHolder should be considered highly suspicious and must be investigated further. Unplanned changes to AdminSDHolder are a very strong indicator of compromise of Active Directory.

Indicator.Name: Dangerous Trust Attribute Set
Indicator.Description: This indicator identifies trusts set with either TRUST_ATTRIBUTE_CROSS_ORGANIZATION_ENABLE_TGT_DELEGATION or TRUST_ATTRIBUTE_PIM_TRUST. These bits will either allow a kerberos ticket to be delegated or reduce the protection that SID Filtering provides.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
An attacker that has compromised a remote domain can spoof any user or machine in the local domain. This can allow the attacker to access any resource as well as escalate their privileges, thus compromising the entire forest.

References

Unfiltered outbound domain trust relationship | ANSSI

Inbound trust relationships with delegation | ANSSI
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} domains with dangerous trust attribute settings.
Indicator.Remediation: Confirm that the trust attributes are configured as desired.

Indicator.Name: Ephemeral Admins
Indicator.Description: This indicator looks for users which were added and removed from an admin group within a 48 hour span of time. Such short-lived accounts may indicate malicious activity.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise: In most environments, management of admin accounts is tightly controlled and audited. This indicator provides a fast method to create a list of ephemeral admins for investigation and review.
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} users who have been added & removed from an admin group within 48 hours.
Indicator.Remediation: Confirm that any additions/removals from admin groups are valid and properly accounted for.

Indicator.Name: Guest accounts that were inactive for more than 30 days
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks for Guest accounts that have not signed in, using an interactive or non-interactive sign in, in the past 30 days.

Required permissions

  • User.Read.All
  • AuditLog.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Guests are a feature of Entra ID that allows for business-to-business (B2B) collaboration with users external to your organization. Commonly used for Microsoft 365 and application sharing with partners, vendors, contractors, and other users who collaborate with the organization but are not employees, a guest user will be represented in the organizations Entra tenant.

Guest users are not members of the organization, they have a different risk profile, and are considered less trusted due to the organization not having full control over and insight into the user object and the devices they come from. Because of the nature of guests, they might become stale, that is, their guest account is no longer used or needed in the Entra tenant.

Unused guest accounts should be cleaned up in an Entra tenant, to ensure that an attacker cannot compromise the guest and access resources in the tenant.

References

Monitor and clean up stale guest accounts - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} Guests that are inactive for more than 30 days
Indicator.Remediation:
Review the guest users indicated in the results, and determine if the guest objects should be deleted from Entra ID. If the guest object needs to be kept for infrequent use, the object can alternatively be disabled, and then re-enabled when the guest needs access again.

If licensed for Entra Identity Governance, organizations can leverage inactive guest insights and configure multi-stage guest reviews in Identity Governance, building a robust lifecycle around guest user objects.

To review guest users, open the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link), expand Identity, expand Users, and select All users.

In the blade on the right, search for the guest object identified. Select the guest user. In the user properties, select Delete towards the top on the right blade to delete the user.

If you want to disable the user, under Account status, select Edit, and in the new blade that opens uncheck Account enabled. Select Save at the bottom.

Deleting active guest users objects in Entra ID will negatively impact the users ability to access resources.

Indicator.Name: Suspicious Directory Synchronization Accounts role member
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks for objects assigned the Directory Synchronization Accounts role that are not the default Entra Connect users.

Required permissions

  • Directory.Read.All
  • RoleManagement.Read.Directory
  • AuditLog.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
The Directory Synchronization Accounts role in Entra ID is reserved for the accounts used for Entra Connect. This directory role is highly privileged and is not meant for assignment to other objects.

An attacker may use this role to perform highly privileged tasks in an Entra tenant, which can result is further lateral movement, data exfiltration, privilege escalation, and creating methods of persistence.

References

Microsoft Entra built-in roles - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} objects that are not default Entra Connect accounts assigned to the Directory Synchronization Account role.
Indicator.Remediation:
Remove the objects from the Directory Synchronization Accounts role assignment.

Role management for Directory Synchronization Accounts cannot be performed from the Entra admin center.

Organizations are responsible for verification of all changes they make to this role. Removal of a Entra connect account from this role may break directory synchronization processes.

There are different methods for connecting to Microsoft Graph to perform operations on this role assignment. These steps us Graph Explorer.

To verify role membership, open Graph Explorer (direct link).

Sign in to the impacted tenant on the right-hand side by selecting the user icon. By default Graph Explorer shows a set of sample tenant data.

Perform a GET against the beta API with the following query:

https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/directoryRoles(roleTemplateId='{{d29b2b05-8046-44ba-8758-1e26182fcf32}}')/members

In the response preview pane, the membership of the Directory Synchronization Accounts role is displayed.

Follow the instructions for Remove member documented here by Microsoft to remove the abnormal user:

Remove directory role member - Microsoft Graph beta | Microsoft Learn

Indicator.Name: Resource Based Constrained Delegation applied to AZUREADSSOACC account
Indicator.Description: This indicator looks for Resource Based Constrained Delegation configured on the Entra Seamless SSO account.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
When configuring Entra Seamless SSO, a computer account is created in Active Directory, AZUREADSSOACC. This computer account is used by Entra ID to decrypt Kerberos tickets that are used as part of the seamless SSO mechanism between Active Directory and Entra ID. If resource-based constrained delegation is configured on the AZUREADSSOACC computer account, an account with the delegation would be able to generate Ticket Granting Service (TGS) requests to Entra ID on behalf of the AZUREADSSOACC account, and impersonate any user in the Entra tenant that is synchronized from Active Directory.

An attacker can use this method to move laterally from Active Directory into Entra ID.

References

Microsoft Entra Connect: Seamless single sign-on - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn

Microsoft Entra Connect: Seamless Single Sign-On - How it works - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} Domain(s) with Resource Based Constrained Delegation configured on AZUREADSSOACC account.
Indicator.Remediation:
Clear the msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity attribute on the AZUREADSSOACC computer account.

There is no functional reason to allow resource-based constrained delegation on this computer account.


To remove, run the following PowerShell command against the computer account:


Set-ADComputer -Identity AZUREADSSOACC$ -Clear "msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity"

Organizations should also investigate the necessity for Entra Seamless SSO. If devices are configured for hybrid join, Seamless SSO is not used. Refer to the reference Microsoft article for more information about SSO with a primary refresh token (PRT) vs. the Seamless SSO system.

Indicator.Name: Check if legacy authentication is allowed
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks if legacy authentication is blocked either by Conditional Access policies or Security Defaults.

Required permissions

  • Policy.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Legacy authentication presents risk that an attacker may use compromised credentials to access resources, as legacy authentication does not support security mechanisms like Conditional Access or MFA.

While Microsoft has now restricted the use of legacy authentication in Entra ID, organizations should ensure it is blocked with their own policies for defense-in-depth.

References

Block legacy authentication with Conditional Access - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Legacy Authentication is not being blocked.
Indicator.Remediation:
Create a Conditional Access policy blocking legacy authentication.

Microsoft provides both a Conditional Access policy template, as well as step-by-step instructions for creating a Conditional Access policy to block legacy authentication.

If legacy authentication is still required for limited resources that may utilize it, such as SMTP with Exchange Online, additional Conditional Access policies should be created for those resources to limit authentication to trusted IP ranges.

Creation and deployment of Conditional Access policies can negatively impact users if not tested. Organizations need to ensure that policies are tested before broad deployment.

Indicator.Name: Administrative units are not being used
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks for the use of administrative units in the Entra tenant.

Required permissions

  • AdministrativeUnit.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Administrative Units are an Entra ID feature that allow for restricting administrative scope of privileged users. Organizations that leverage administrative units can have more granularity in role assignment. As an example, an organization may create different administrative units for different geographical locations, and then assign the Helpdesk Administrator role on a regional basis, instead of across the entire Entra tenant.

An attacker will target privileged user accounts to abuse the privileges assigned to them. By using administrative units, an organization can limit the surface area of attack.

References

Administrative units in Microsoft Entra ID - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: There are ZERO Administrative Units created.
Indicator.Remediation:
Review the organizational structure and current role assignments, and consider leveraging administrative units to limit privileged user scope.

Like organizational units in Active Directory, administrative unit design and implementation will be specific to the organizations structure.

Administrative units can be configured in the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link). They are located by expanding Identity, expanding Roles & admins and selecting Admin units.

Indicator.Name: Security defaults not enabled
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks whether security defaults are enabled when there are no Conditional Access policies configured.

Required permissions

  • Policy.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
As attackers constantly attempt to compromise cloud environments, it is important to maintain the highest possible security baseline for authentication. To protect the authentication process and privileged actions, security defaults are recommended for tenants that have no conditional access policies configured. Security defaults will require MFA, block legacy authentication, and require additional authentication when accessing the Azure portal, Azure Powershell, or the Azure CLI.

References

Providing a default level of security in Microsoft Entra ID - Microsoft Entra | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: There are ZERO Conditional Access Policies enabled and Security Defaults are not configured.
Indicator.Remediation:
Enable security defaults or Conditional Access policies.

If the organization is licensed for Entra ID P1 or higher, or associated EMS or M365 licensing, it is preferred to use Conditional Access for securing authentication.

If the licensing is not available, then an organization should enable security defaults.

To enable, open the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link), expand Identity, expand Users, and select Overview.

In the blade on the right, select Properties. Under the Security defaults section towards the bottom, select Manage security defaults. Set Security defaults to Enabled. Select Save.

If Conditional Access is currently used by the organization, it should not enable Security defaults.

Indicator.Name: MFA not configured for privileged accounts
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks that MFA (Multi-factor Authentication) is enabled for users assigned privileged roles.

Required permissions

  • RoleManagement.Read.Directory
  • Reports.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Users that hold privileged roles in Entra ID are high-value targets for an attacker. It is critical for organizations to require MFA for all users holding privileged roles.

The risk associated with compromise of a privileged user is equal to the permissions and rights held within the role assigned to the user.

Implementing changes to Conditional Access policies and enabling Security Defaults can negatively impact users if not properly tested.

References

Secure your Microsoft Entra identity infrastructure - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn

Require MFA for administrators with Conditional Access - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn

Providing a default level of security in Microsoft Entra ID - Microsoft Entra | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: {0} privileged user(s) found without MFA configured.
Indicator.Remediation:
Users identified as impacted must be configured to require MFA at minimum for authentication in Entra ID.

Break-glass accounts may be an exception to MFA requirements, and can be added to an ignore list for this indicator. Organizations need to monitor their break-glass accounts to ensure that the accounts are not used without awareness

Microsoft provides both a Conditional Access policy template, as well as step-by-step instructions for creating a Conditional Access Policy requiring MFA for administrators. If a Conditional Access policy already exists for privileged users, the identified users can be added to that policy.

Organizations that do not have licensing for Conditional Access policies should enable Security Defaults.

Indicator.Name: Unrestricted user consent allowed
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks if users are allowed to add applications from unverified publishers to Entra ID.

Required permissions

  • Policy.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Publisher verification is a process from Microsoft to verify the authenticity of applications from 3rd party developers and the developers organization.

Attackers use attacks such as Illicit Consent Grants, also known as OAuth consent phishing, to compromise user accounts. In these attacks, the attacker tricks a user to consent to a malicious application that is not verified by Microsoft.

References

Configure how users consent to applications - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn

Publisher verification overview - Microsoft identity platform | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Users are allowed to consent to risky 3rd party apps.
Indicator.Remediation:
Do not allow users to consent to applications from unverified publishers.

To disable consent for all applications, open the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link), expand Identity, expand Applications, and select Enterprise applications.

In the blade on the right, under Security select Consent and permissions. In the new blade that opens, under User consent settings ensure that Allow user consent for apps is not selected.

Organizations must understand that changes they make to the application consent process can impact users onboarding new applications, and the organizations must communicate out the changes as needed to impacted users.

Indicator.Name: Conditional Access Policy that does not require a password change from high risk users
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks whether a conditional access policy exists that requires a password change for users marked as high-risk by Entra ID Protection.

Required permissions

  • Policy.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Entra ID Protection uses several signals to determine the risk level of a user in Entra ID. If there is a strong indication that a user account has been compromised, Entra ID Protection will mark that user risk as high.

An attacker may compromise a user account in different ways, such as with targeted password spray attacks or attacker-in-the-middle (AiTM) scenarios seen with phishing. Entra ID Protection can also find and validate leaked credentials on the internet, and match them to credentials in the Entra tenant. If an account is verified compromised, the compromise of the account should be remediated.

References

User risk-based password change - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: There isn't a Conditional Access Policy configured that requires a password change for high risk users.
Indicator.Remediation:
Deploy a Conditional Access policy requiring password change for high-risk users.

Microsoft provides both a Conditional Access policy template, as well as step-by-step instructions for creating a Conditional Access Policy requiring password change for high-risk users.

Implementing changes to Conditional Access policies can negatively impact users if not properly tested. Organizations need to verify all policy and security changes and communicate them out accordingly.

Indicator.Name: Foreign Security Principals in Privileged Group
Indicator.Description: This indicator looks for members of privileged groups which are Foreign Security Principals. Special care should be taken when including accounts from other domains as members of privileged groups.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
While not immediately indicative of an attack, privileged users that are not clearly marked as such (adminCount =1) represent an exposure in that they may be used nefariously without being detected. Since Foreign Security Principals do not have the adminCount attribute, they could miss being detected by some security auditing tools. Additionally, an attacker may add a privileged account and attempt to hide it using this method.

References

audit-user-account-management of Microsoft
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} Foreign Security Principals as members of privileged groups.
Indicator.Remediation: Validate whether this member should be a member of the privileged group.

Indicator.Name: Privileged group contains guest account
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks whether any privileged roles have been assigned to guest accounts.

Required permissions

  • User.Read.All
  • RoleManagement.Read.Directory
  • AuditLog.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Guests are a feature of Entra ID that allows for business-to-business (B2B) collaboration with users external to your organization. Commonly used for Microsoft 365 and application sharing with partners, vendors, contractors, and other users who collaborate with the organization but are not employees, a guest user will be represented in the organizations Entra tenant. Guest users are not members of the organization, have a different risk profile, and are considered less trusted due to the organization not having full control over and insight into the user object and the devices they come from. Under majority circumstances, guests should not be allowed to invite other guests into the Entra tenant.

An attacker may target a guest account for compromise, and use the associated privileges to further compromise the Entra tenant and associated services. The attack surface is relative to the role assigned to the guest.

References

Create an access review of Azure resource and Microsoft Entra roles in PIM - Microsoft Entra ID Governance | Microsoft Learn

NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5
Indicator.ResultMessage: {0} privileged user(s) found as members of privileged groups.
Indicator.Remediation:
Review the guest users identified and their assigned roles in the Entra tenant, and determine if they should continue to hold these unused roles. The organization should fully understand the associated risk involved with the guest holding the role assignment. A privileged access strategy should follow a governance plan for assigning least privilege, such as that documented in NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5.

You can review existing role assignments in the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link).

Organizations should use the Access Reviews feature of Entra Privileged Identity Management to help manage the lifecycle of privileged role assignments.

Indicator.Name: Check for guests having permission to invite other guests
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks for guest users that have permission to invite other guests.

Required permissions

  • Policy.Read.All
  • AuditLog.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Guests are a feature of Entra ID that allows for business-to-business (B2B) collaboration with users external to your organization. Commonly used for Microsoft 365 and application sharing with partners, vendors, contractors, and other users who collaborate with the organization but are not employees. Guest users are not members of the organization, they have a different risk profile, and are considered less trusted due to the organization not having full control over and insight into the user object and the devices they come from.

An attacker may target a guest account for compromise, and use the privilege to invite guests into the Entra tenant to further create persistence and move laterally.

References

Enable B2B external collaboration settings - Microsoft Entra External ID | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Guests are allowed to send guest invitations.
Indicator.Remediation:
Change guest invite restrictions from the setting Anyone in the organization can invite guest users including guests and non-admins (most-inclusive) in the Entra tenant.

To change, open the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link), expand Identity, expand External Identities, and select External collaboration settings.

In the blade on the right, change the radio dial selection under Guest invite settings, Guest invite restrictions.

Organizations need to make a determination on what guest invite restriction setting to select, as they have different levels of restrictions on both users and guests. Ensure that any changes are communicated out, as they can impact existing business processes.

Select Save at the top of the blade.

Indicator.Name: Check for risky API permissions granted to application service principals
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks if one of the following risky application permissions have been granted to a service principal in Microsoft Graph: RoleManagement.ReadWrite.Directory, AppRoleAssignment.ReadWrite.All

Required permissions

  • Application.Read.All
  • AuditLog.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Application permissions in Microsoft Graph allow a service principal, which is used by an application, to act on its own behalf. Credentials, either as secrets or certificates, can be assigned to a service principal, and used to authenticate as the application and interact with Microsoft Graph.

Certain application permissions in Entra ID are designed to allow for the bypass of normal permission consent processes that would require interaction from a highly privileged user. The permissions are RoleManagement.ReadWrite.Directory and AppRoleAssignment.ReadWrite.All can allow an application to promote itself to a highly privileged role, including Global Administrator.

An attacker can use these privileges for dangerous privilege escalation in Entra ID.

References

Review permissions granted to applications - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn

App consent grant investigation | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Risky permissions exist on a configured application.
Indicator.Remediation:
Review the indicated applications to determine if the risky permissions are necessary. If the permissions are determined unnecessary, they should be removed.

To review permissions on the application, open the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link), expand Identity, expand Applications, and select Enterprise Applications.

In the blade on the right, search for the application by name. Select the application.

In the new blade that opens, under Security select Permissions. The permissions for the application will be shown on the blade. To revoke application permissions, select Review permissions at the top menu.

Removing consented application permissions on both single-tenant and multi-tenant service principals may result in degraded or broken functionality of the application. Organizations must understand the applications needs before removing.

Indicator.Name: Check for users with weak or no MFA
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks all users for their registered MFA types.

Required permissions

  • Reports.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
With the rise of sophisticated phishing attacks, organizations should strive to move users towards strong MFA mechanisms. Modern attacker-in-the-middle (AiTM) phishing systems can defeat SMS text and voice call methods of MFA. While less common, phone numbers are susceptible to SIM swapping attacks, allowing an attacker to takeover a users phone number, and then receive the MFA call or text on the malicious phone.

References

Authentication Methods Activity - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn

How to run a registration campaign to set up Microsoft Authenticator - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: There are Entra users which are not MFA registered or who have only weaker authentication methods configured.
Indicator.Remediation:
Examine the feasibility of requiring users to use a strong form of MFA.

The Authentication methods activity report in Entra ID can provide usage details on MFA methods for users in the Entra tenant.

To view, open the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link), expand Identity, expand Show more, expand Monitoring & health, select Usage & insights. In the right blade, select Authentication methods activity.

Users may have weaker forms of MFA registered, but are not actively using them, and should be evaluated to remove them. A registration campaign can help users in the organization enroll in the Microsoft Authenticator App for MFA.

To configure a registration campaign, open the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link), expand Protection, select Authentication methods. In the right blade, select Registration campaign.

Organizations need to understand the risk of users not having MFA enabled on their accounts. If MFA is not feasible, organizations should determine if other compensating controls can be put in place, such as network or device compliance restrictions through Conditional Access policies.

Indicator.Name: SSO computer account with password last set over 90 days ago
Indicator.Description: This indicator checks the Entra Seamless Single Sign-On computer account, AZUREADSSOACC, to determine if the password has been rotated in the last 90 days.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
When configuring Entra Seamless SSO, a computer account is created in Active Directory, AZUREADSSOACC. This computer account is used by Entra ID to decrypt Kerberos tickets that are used as part of the seamless SSO mechanism between Active Directory and Entra ID. Because this is not an actual computer that is a member of Active Directory, the password for it cannot automatically be rolled over every 30 days. If an attacker compromises this account, they can generate Ticket Granting Service (TGS) request to Entra ID and impersonate any user in the Entra tenant that is synchronized from Active Directory.

An attacker can use this to move laterally from Active Directory into Entra ID.

References

Microsoft Entra Connect SSO FAQ - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn

Microsoft Entra Connect: Seamless single sign-on - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn

Managed service accounts with passwords unchanged for more than 90 days | ANSSI
Indicator.ResultMessage: The computer account used for Azure SSO has not changed in the last {1} days.
Indicator.Remediation:
The Kerberos decryption key used for Entra Seamless Single Sign-On must be rolled.


For details on the process of rolling the key, refer to the Microsoft reference article.

Organizations should also investigate the necessity for Entra Seamless SSO. If devices are configured for hybrid join, Seamless SSO is not used. Refer to the reference Microsoft article for more information about SSO with a primary refresh token (PRT) vs. the Seamless SSO system.

Indicator.Name: Conditional Access Policy does not require MFA on privileged accounts
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks whether Conditional Access policies require mutli-factor authentication (MFA) for privileged accounts.

Required permissions

  • Policy.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Users that hold privileged roles in Entra ID are high-value targets for an attacker. It is critical for organizations to require MFA for all users holding privileged roles.

The risk associated with compromise of a privileged user is equal to the permissions and rights held within the role assigned to the user.

References

Secure your Microsoft Entra identity infrastructure - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn

Require MFA for administrators with Conditional Access - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Conditional Access policies do not require all privileged roles to use MFA
Indicator.Remediation:
Users identified as impacted must have a Conditional Access policy applied enforcing MFA.

Break-glass accounts may be an exception to MFA requirements, and can be added to an ignore list for this indicator. Organizations need to monitor their break-glass accounts to ensure that the accounts are not used without awareness.

Microsoft provides both a Conditional Access policy template, as well as step-by-step instructions for creating a Conditional Access Policy requiring MFA for administrators.

If a Conditional Access policy already exists for privileged users, the identified users or roles can be added to that policy instead.

The policy implemented should implement MFA for the following roles:

Application administrator, Application Developer, Authentication administrator, Authentication Extensibility Administrator, Authentication Policy Administrator, B2C IEF Keyset Administrator, Billing administrator, Cloud application administrator, Cloud Device Administrator, Conditional Access administrator, Directory Synchronization Accounts, Directory Writers, Domain Name Administrator, Exchange administrator, External Identity Provider Administrator, Global administrator, Global Reader, Helpdesk administrator, Hybrid Identity Administrator, Intune Administrator, Lifecycle Workflows Administrator, Partner Tier1 Support, Partner Tier2 Support, Password administrator, Privileged authentication administrator, Privileged Role Administrator, Security administrator, Security Operator, Security Reader, SharePoint administrator, User administrator.

Additional privileged roles can be added if desired.

Implementing changes to Conditional Access policies and enabling Security Defaults can both negatively impact users if not properly tested. Organizations need to verify all policy and security changes and communicate them out accordingly.

Indicator.Name: Entra Connect sync account password reset
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks if there have been any password resets against Entra Connect sync Sync_ accounts not logged on in the past three days, which are considered stale.

Required permissions

  • AuditLog.Read.All
  • Directory.Read.All
  • User.Read.All
  • RoleManagement.Read.Directory
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Entra Connect uses a special user account in Entra ID as a service account for synchronization of users from Active Directory. These accounts hold a highly-privileged role in Entra ID, which can be used by an attacker for lateral movement, privilege escalation, and other forms of compromise is Entra ID.

Entra Connect accounts should have continual authentication traffic, as sync cycles occur every 30 minutes. An account that has not been logged into over three days could be considered stale. Unused accounts have a higher chance of going unnoticed if compromised.
Indicator.ResultMessage: There have been {0} password change(s) on Azure AD Connect synchronization accounts which have not logged on within the past 3 days.
Indicator.Remediation:
Review the accounts listed in the results of this indicator.

If the Entra Connect server was offline for known reasons and the password was recently changed by the organization running a password rotation on the account, then this result might be expected.

If the account listed is associated with a no longer used Entra Connect server, it should be deleted from Entra ID.

If this account is believed to be suspicious, the Audit logs for the account activity can be viewed in Entra ID.

To view the audit logs, open the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link), expand Identity, expand Users, and select All users.

In the blade on the right, search for the user indicated in the results. Select the user account.

In the user blade that opens, select Audit logs. The audit log details of changes made will be shown.

If the account is believed to be compromised, organizations must perform a thorough audit analysis of the user account

Removing an active Entra Connect synchronization user account will break synchronization

Indicator.Name: Conditional Access policies that contain MFA Trusted IPs
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks if any enabled Conditional Access policies contain references to MFA Trusted IPs.

Required permissions

  • Policy.Read.All
  • AuditLog.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
MFA Trusted IPs is a legacy mechanism originally designed for specifying IP addresses to exclude from per-user MFA. Conditional Access policies also have the ability to refer to these IP address ranges as Multifactor authentication trusted IPs in the Locations condition. Newer security mechanisms, like Continuous Access Evaluation (CAE), help enforce Conditional Access policies that are location-based by IP address or range. Continuous Access Evaluation is not capable of using IP ranges specified in MFA Trusted IPs.

An organizations using MFA Trusted IPs may see behavior of Conditional Access policies and Continuous Access Evaluation working as intended, the security posture of target users being lower than expected, and prone to a larger surface area of attack.

References

Continuous access evaluation in Microsoft Entra ID - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn

Network in Conditional Access policy - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn

Configure Microsoft Entra multifactor authentication - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: There are Conditional Access policies referencing legacy MFA Trusted IPs.
Indicator.Remediation:
IP addresses and ranges in Multifactor authentication trusted IPs should be creates as a Named location to be used by the impacted Conditional Access policies.

To configure, open the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link), expand Protection, expand Conditional Access, and select Named locations.

To view existing trusted IPs, select the Configure multifactor authentication trusted IPs (direct link) link on the upper portion of the right blade, then click on Service settings. A new named location should be created containing the same ranges.

Creation and deployment of Conditional Access policies can negatively impact users if not tested. Organizations need to ensure that policies are tested before broad deployment.

Organizations that are using AD FS with Entra Multifactor authentication, per-user MFA, or Entra MFA server may need to retain their trusted IP settings.

Indicator.Name: Global Administrators that signed in during the last 14 days
Indicator.Description:
This indicator looks for Global Administrators that have signed in during the past 14 days.

Required permissions

  • RoleManagement.Read.All
  • User.Read.All
  • AuditLog.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Users that hold the Global Administrator role are the most privileged users in Entra ID. Organizations should work to limit the necessity of using a Global Administrator, such as using less permissive roles when possible.

An attacker will find users with the Global Administrator role as high-valued, and a compromised Global Administrator can lead to several attacks against the organization, that can include data exfiltration, lateral movement into other systems in Azure, multi-cloud, and on-premise systems, and can result in complete tenant compromise where an organization becomes locked out of their Entra tenant by the attacker.

References

Microsoft Entra built-in roles - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} from {1} Global Administrators that signed in (interactive or non interactive) in the last 14 days.
Indicator.Remediation:
Review the Global Administrators listed in the results of this indicator.

If an organization believes that there is highly suspicious activity or a confirmed compromise of a Global Administrator, they must treat this as a critical security event.

If this account is believed to be suspicious, the Audit logs for the account activity can be viewed in Entra ID.

To view the audit logs, open the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link), expand Identity, expand Users, and select All users.

In the blade on the right, search for the user indicated in the results. Select the user account.

In the user blade that opens, select Audit logs. The audit log details of changes made by the user will be shown.

Indicator.Name: Privileged user credentials cached on RODC
Indicator.Description: This indicator checks for privileged user credentials that are cached to RODCs.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
In many instances, read-only domain controllers (RODCs) are deployed in area where there is a lower level of physical security to the servers.

Attackers may try to target read-only domain controllers due to the lower level of security around these. While it is not a direct indicator of an attack, credentials for privileged users should not be cached on RODCs in the case the domain controller is compromised.

References

RODC Features | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} privileged users with credentials cached to an RODC.
Indicator.Remediation:
It is recommended to adjust the password replication policy on the RODC to deny replication of credentials for the identified privileged users.

You can review the current settings by opening Active Directory Users and Computers, locate the identified RODC, and then right-click and select Properties.

In the Properties of the RODC, select the Password Replication Policy tab.

Review the replication policies, identify the policy that is permitting replication of credentials, and adjust to ensure there is a deny policy for the privileged users.

Indicator.Name: Conditional Access Policy that disable admin token persistence
Indicator.Description:
This indicator looks for Conditional Access policies that disable token persistence for users with admin roles and have a sign-in frequency that is less than or equal to 9 hours.

Required permissions

  • Policy.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Sign-in frequency (SIF) is a Conditional Access policy setting that will invalidate the refresh tokens for a user after a defined period of time. While sign-in frequency can have negative impact on personas of enterprise users, it serves as a defense-in-depth tool for protecting privileged users.

Privileged users are high-value targets for attackers, and one modern attack mechanism is token theft and token replay, allowing an attacker to impersonate the user if their tokens are stolen from their device.

References

Configure authentication session management - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: No Conditional Access policies are configured to disable admin token persistence with a sign-in frequency set to 9 or less hours.
Indicator.Remediation:
Deploy a Conditional Access policy that enforces sign-in frequency for privileged users.

To create a Conditional Access policy, open the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link), expand Protection and select Conditional Access.

In the blade on the right, select Policies. Select New Policy. A new policy should be created that is scoped to privileged roles with a time interval of 9 hours or lower:

Application administrator, Application Developer, Authentication administrator, Authentication Extensibility Administrator, Authentication Policy Administrator, B2C IEF Keyset Administrator, Billing administrator, Cloud application administrator, Cloud Device Administrator, Conditional Access administrator, Directory Synchronization Accounts, Directory Writers, Domain Name Administrator, Exchange administrator, External Identity Provider Administrator, Global administrator, Global Reader, Helpdesk administrator, Hybrid Identity Administrator, Intune Administrator, Lifecycle Workflows Administrator, Partner Tier1 Support, Partner Tier2 Support, Password administrator, Privileged authentication administrator, Privileged Role Administrator, Security administrator, Security Operator, Security Reader, SharePoint administrator, User administrator.

Conditional Access policies can negatively impact user authentication, and organizations must verify their policies before deployment.

Indicator.Name: Smart card password rotation disabled
Indicator.Description: This indicator checks whether the mcDS-ExpirePasswordsOnSmartCardOnlyAccounts attribute, which determines if smart card-only accounts must follow regular password rotation schedules, is disabled.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
The msDS-ExpirePasswordsOnSmartCardOnlyAccounts attribute is a security configuration that determines if smart card-only accounts must follow regular password rotation schedules. When enabled, it ensures these specialized accounts comply with the domain's password policy. This security feature is available in domains operating at Windows Server 2016 functional level or higher.

While not immediately indicative of an attack, when disabled, smart card-only accounts can maintain the same password indefinitely, potentially creating a security vulnerability that attackers can exploit for persistent access and lateral movement.

References

Attribute msDS-ExpirePasswordsOnSmartCardOnlyAccounts

Missing password expiration for smart card users | ANSSI
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} domains with msDS-ExpirePasswordsOnSmartCardOnlyAccounts disabled.
Indicator.Remediation: To remediate this security issue, enable the msDS-ExpirePasswordsOnSmartCardOnlyAccounts attribute by opening ADSIEdit.msc with an account that has domain administrative privileges. Connect to the Configuration naming context, navigate to the domain configuration object, right-click on the domain object, and select "Properties". Go to the "Attribute Editor" tab, locate the "msDS-ExpirePasswordsOnSmartCardOnlyAccounts" attribute, and change its value to "TRUE" (case-sensitive). Click "Apply" and then "OK" to save the changes.

Indicator.Name: Operators Groups that are not empty
Indicator.Description: This indicator checks if the Account Operators, Server Operators, Backup Operators and Print Operators groups in Active Directory are populated.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
The operator groups in Active Directory, Account Operators, Server Operators, Backup Operators, and Print Operators, all provide users within these groups certain privileges over different critical domain resources in Active Directory and different levels of access to domain controllers.

An attacker may target users in these groups for further privilege escalation and lateral movement in Active Directory.

References

Active Directory security groups | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} Members in Operator groups
Indicator.Remediation:
Organizations should evaluate the use of these groups, and work to reduce and remove membership of these groups.

Organizations that are using these groups should implement an appropriate rights delegation to the necessary OUs in Active Directory as an alternative. For Backup Operators, organizations should implement a different group and manage access to lower tier servers or workstations through group policy objects.

If the organization accepts the risk of these groups being populated, they should use attack path analysis software, such as Forest Druid, to determine the attack paths that exist due to the population of these groups, and further consider members of this group to be Tier 0.

Indicator.Name: RC4 or DES encryption type are supported by Domain Controllers
Indicator.Description: This indicator checks if RC4 or DES encryption is supported by Domain Controllers
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
RC4 and DES are considered an insecure form of encryption, susceptible to various cryptographic attacks. Multiple vulnerabilities in the RC4 and DES algorithms allow MitM and deciphering attacks.

References

CVE-2013-2566

CVE-2015-2808

DC/RODC supported encryption algorithms | ANSSI
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} Domain Controllers that support RC4 or DES encryption
Indicator.Remediation: It is best practice to disable support for RC4 and DES on domain controllers. Proceed with caution, as this can cause clients that request RC4 encrypted kerberos tickets by default to fail. Disable it by adding the group policy Network security: Configure encryption types allowed for Kerberos and select only AES-128, AES-256 encryption types, to a GPO that affects the Domain Controllers container. The group policy path is Computer Configuration\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options.

Indicator.Name: Primary users with SPN not supporting AES encryption on Kerberos
Indicator.Description: This indicator shows all Primary users with SPNs that do not support AES-128 or AES-256 encryption type.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
AES encryption is stronger than RC4 encryption. Configuring primary users with SPN to support AES encryption will not mitigate attacks such as Kerberoasting but does force AES by default, meaning that it is possible to monitor for encryption downgrade attacks to RC4 (Kerberoasting attacks)

References

Network security: Configure encryption types allowed for Kerberos | Microsoft Learn

Service accounts supported encryption algorithms | ANSSI

Kerberos preauthentication disabled | ANSSI
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} Primary users with SPN not supporting AES encryption
Indicator.Remediation: Best practice is to enable AES encryption support on service accounts.

Indicator.Name: LDAP signing is not required on Domain Controllers
Indicator.Description: This indicator checks for domain controllers where LDAP signing is not required.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Unsigned network traffic is exposed to MiTM attacks, where attackers alter packets and forward them to the LDAP server, causing the server to make decisions based on forged requests from the LDAP client.

References

Domain controller LDAP server signing requirements - Windows 10 | Microsoft Learn

Network security LDAP client signing requirements - Windows 10 | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} DCs that do not require LDAP Signing.
Indicator.Remediation:
To remediate follow the steps below:

If the steps are not followed in order, disruption to Active Directory may occur.

  1. Configure clients to request LDAP signing. Group policy: Network security:LDAP client signing requirements, select Negotiate signing.
  2. When all clients request signing, configure domain controllers to require LDAP signing. Group policy: Domain Controller:LDAP server signing requirements, select Require signing.
  3. Configure clients to require LDAP signing. Group policy: Network security:LDAP client signing requirements, select Require signing.
  4. For full details on the process please see the reference articles.

Indicator.Name: SMB Signing is not required on Domain Controllers
Indicator.Description: This indicator looks for domain controllers where SMB signing is not required.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Unsigned network traffic is susceptible to attacks abusing the NTLM challenge-response protocol. A common example of such attacks is SMB Relay, where an attacker is positioned between the client and the server in order to capture data packets transmitted between the two, thus gaining unauthorized access to the server or other servers on the network.

References

Configure SMB Signing with Confidence
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} DCs that do not require SMB Signing.
Indicator.Remediation:
The following Group Policies need to be enabled in order to enforce SMB Signing on DCs:

  1. Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options > Microsoft network server: Digitally sign communications (always): This policy controls whether the server providing SMB required signing. It determines if SMB signing will have to be negotiated prior to further communication.
  2. Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options > Microsoft network server: Digitally sign communications (if client agrees): This policy determines if SMB server will negotiate SMB signing with clients that request it.

Indicator.Name: SMBv1 is enabled on Domain Controllers
Indicator.Description: This indicator looks for domain controllers where SMBv1 protocol is enabled.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
SMBv1 is an old protocol, considered unsafe and susceptible to all kinds of attacks. It was publicly deprecated by Microsoft in 2014.

References

Configure SMB Signing with Confidence
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} DCs with SMBv1 Enabled.
Indicator.Remediation: Microsoft recommends to disable SMBv1 whenever possible on both client and server side. Do note, before disabling SMBv1 and to avoid additional errors, make sure best practices are followed regarding the usage of deprecated OS (Windows 2000, 2003, XP, CE), network printers using SMBv1 scan2shares functionalities, or software accessing Windows share with a custom implementation relying on SMBv1.

Indicator.Name: Certificate templates with 3 or more insecure configurations
Indicator.Description: This indicator checks if certificate templates in the forest have a minimum of three insecure configurations - Manager approval is disabled, No authorized signatures are required, SAN enabled, Authentication EKU present.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
The following configurations of a certificate template can be exploited by adversaries:
  1. Manager approval is disabled - new certificates are automatically approved if the user has the correct enrollment rights.
  2. No authorized signatures are required - CSRs (Certificate Signing Requests) are not signed by any existing authorized certificate.
  3. SAN (Subject Alternative Name) Enabled - Allowing the creator of a certificate template to specify the subjectAltName in the CSR, thus they can make the request as anyone, even a domain admin.
  4. Authentication EKU (Enhanced Key Usage) present - if present, the EKU created from the certificate template will allow the user to authenticate with it.

References

NIST-SP1800-16B

Dangerous enrollment permission on authentication certificate templates | ANSSI
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} certificate templates that can potentially be abused.
Indicator.Remediation:
Multiple actions can be taken to ensure certificate templates will be less vulnerable:
  1. Enable manager approval - make sure manager approval is enabled and required on the certificate, and approve each request manually after inspecting it.
