  2. No authorized signatures are required - it is recommended to set it to 1 so that each request will have to be signed by an authorized certificate.
  3. SAN Enabled - evaluate if the certificate needs to specify a subjectAltName, if not disable this option.
  4. Authentication EKU present - make sure the certificate template is being used for authentication only.
    For example, a certificate that is solely used for code signing should not also be used for authentication.

Indicator.Name: Certificate templates that allow requesters to specify a subjectAltName
Indicator.Description: This indicator checks if certificate templates are enabling requesters to specify a subjectAltName in the CSR.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
When certificate templates allow requesters to specify a subjectAltName in the CSR, the result is that they can request a certificate as anyone. For example, a domain admin. When that is combined with an authentication EKU present in the certificate template it can become extremely dangerous.

References

NIST-SP1800-16B

Dangerous enrollment permission on authentication certificate templates | ANSSI
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} certificate templates that allow SAN
Indicator.Remediation: Ensure that when a SAN is allowed on a certificate template it is absolutely required on the template, so that the certificate must specify a subjectAltName. If not absolutely required, it should be disabled. This configuration can be viewed under the "Supply in request" option in the "Subject Name" tab in certtmpl.msc. When an authentication EKU is also present on the certificate template this becomes very dangerous and action should be taken to disable SAN on it.

Indicator.Name: Entra ID privileged users that are also privileged in AD
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks for Entra ID privileged users that are also privileged in on-premise AD.

Required permissions

  • RoleManagement.Read.Directory
  • RoleManagement.Read.All
  • Directory.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
It is common in organizations that the people who manage Active Directory also manage Entra ID, and potentially M365 and Azure as well. In these scenarios users may require privileged credentials in all of these environments. It is critical for organizations to separate the privileged credentials out between the different identity stores, so that one user is not privileged in multiple directory systems.

If a user holds privileges in Active Directory and Entra ID, and the account becomes compromised by an attacker, an attacker can move laterally between the two directory systems, easily compromising both and the resources attached to them. If the user holds highly privileged roles such as Domain Administrator and Global Administrator, attack damage from compromise can be severe.

References

Best practices for Microsoft Entra roles - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn

Secure access practices for administrators in Microsoft Entra ID - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} Privileged Entra ID users that are also privileged in AD.
Indicator.Remediation:
Review the users identified as holding privileges in both Active Directory and Entra ID, and device a plan for separating out the privileged roles into multiple credentials.

Separation of privileges is usually handled on a case-by-case basis, formulated by the level of privileges the role holds, as well as determining which identity system will be easier to create a new set of privileged credentials for the user in.

A common practice for privilege separation into new sets of privileges users, is to create a new user in the target system that holds the same roles, and start using that user in the target system, validating that role functionality continues as needed. Then, the privileges are removed from the existing user that are no longer needed.

If a privileged user from Active Directory was holding privileges in Entra ID and those privileges have been removed from Entra ID, the organization should also consider if they still need to synchronize the user from Active Directory. This is to defend the user account from potential attacks that target moving laterally between the two directory systems.

If a new privileged user is created in Entra ID, the user should be created as a cloud-only account that is not synchronized from Active Directory, even if it has no privileges in Active Directory. This is to defend the user account from potential attacks that target moving laterally between the two directory systems.

Indicator.Name: Non-admin users can register custom applications
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks for an authorization policy that allows non-admin users to register applications in your Entra tenant.

Required permissions

  • Policy.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
By default all users allowed to register applications in an Entra tenant. While app registrations themselves are not malicious, applications can be granted permissions to access user data with delegated permissions, and admin consent grants can provide the applications with broader sets of permissions.

An attacker may attempt to leverage a breached user account by registering an application, using the application for multiple purposes, such as attempting to move laterally, providing a mechanism for persistence, reconnaissance, and data exfiltration.

References

Delegate application management administrator permissions - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn

Default user permissions - Microsoft Entra | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Every user can register an application.
Indicator.Remediation:
Disable Users can register applications in the Entra tenant.

To disable, open the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link), expand Identity, expand Users, and select User settings.

In the blade on the right, toggle Users can register applications to no.

Select Save at the bottom of the blade.

Organizations should understand how this change impacts their users and communicate the change out according to defined business processes.

Indicator.Name: Guest users are not restricted
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks if guest users are restricted in the Entra tenant.

Required permissions

  • Policy.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Guests are a feature of Entra ID that allows for business-to-business (B2B) collaboration with users external to your organization. Commonly used for Microsoft 365 and application sharing with partners, vendors, contractors, and other users who collaborate with the organization but are not employees, a guest user will be represented in the organizations Entra tenant. Guest users are not members of the organization, have a different risk profile, and are considered less trusted due to the organization not having full control over and insight into the user object and the devices they come from. Under majority circumstances, guests should be restricted in what kind of directory data they can read in the Entra tenant.

An attacker may target a guest account for compromise, and then use the compromised guest to enumerate users and other objects in Entra ID.

References

Restrict guest user access permissions - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Guest users are not restricted in this tenant.
Indicator.Remediation:
Change guest user access restrictions to Guest user access is restricted to properties and memberships of their own directory objects (most restrictive).

To change, open the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link), expand Identity, expand External Identities, and select External collaboration settings.

In the blade on the right, change the radio dial selection under Guest user access restrictions to Guest user access is restricted to properties and memberships of their own directory objects (most restrictive). Select Save at the top of the blade.

Organizations should understand that this will change behavior for guest users in the Entra tenant, and that the changes should be verified and tested, as well as communicated out to impacted users as needed.

Indicator.Name: AD privileged users that are synced to Entra ID
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks for AD privileged users that are synced to Entra ID.

Required permissions

  • User.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
During deployments of Entra Connect or Entra Cloud Sync, organizations may intentionally or unintentionally synchronize privileged users from Active Directory to Entra ID. When this occurs, the attack surface is increased on these privileged users.

Organizations should not synchronize privileged users from Active Directory to Entra ID unless there is a specific and designed intention, and the organization understands the risk of the increased attack surface.

An attacker may attempt to compromise the user account in Entra ID, and then use that compromise to move laterally into Active Directory.

References

Microsoft Entra Connect Sync: Configure filtering - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn

Microsoft Entra Cloud Sync new agent configuration - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} Privileged AD users that are synced to Entra ID.
Indicator.Remediation:
Review the users identified as holding privileges in Active Directory, and remove them from synchronization in Entra Connect or Entra cloud sync.

In Entra Connect, user filtering should be configured on an Organizational unit level. This requires modification of the filtering on the Entra Connect server, as well as any staging servers. Details on adjusting the OU filtering can be found in the reference Microsoft documentation.

In Entra cloud sync, user filtering is configured with scoping filters. Details on adjusting scoping filters can be found in the reference Microsoft documentation.

Organizations must ensure that changes to synchronized users are understood and verified that they will not negatively impact the target users, and any impacted users should have the changes communicated as needed.

Indicator.Name: More than 5 Global Administrators exist
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks for the presence of 5 or more Global Administrators. Please note: If a given user has both an active and eligible assignment for a given role, the user will appear twice in the results.

Required permissions

  • RoleManagement.Read.Directory
  • RoleManagement.Read.All
  • Directory.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Users that hold the Global Administrator role are the most privileged users in Entra ID. Organizations should limit the number of Global Administrators to less than five.

An attacker will find users with the Global Administrator role as high-value, and a compromised Global Administrator can lead to several attacks against the organization, that can include data exfiltration, lateral movement into other systems in Azure, multi-cloud, and on-premise systems, and can result in complete tenant compromise where an organization becomes locked out of their Entra tenant by the attacker.

References

Create an access review of Azure resource and Microsoft Entra roles in PIM - Microsoft Entra ID Governance | Microsoft Learn

Best practices for Microsoft Entra roles - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn

Manage emergency access admin accounts - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} Global Administrators in Azure AD.
Indicator.Remediation:
Review the users assigned the Global Administrator role, and determine if the role assignment can be removed.


To review and manage role assignments, open the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link), expand Identity, select Show more, expand Roles & admins, and select Roles & admins.

In the blade on the right, find the Global Administrator role. Select the role.

From this view you can remove users as necessary from the role. Organizations that have Entra ID P2 licensing available should use access reviews to manage the lifecycle of privileged users.

Organizations must ensure that any removal of the Global Administrator role does not impact the ability to manage their Entra tenant.

Indicator.Name: Guest invites not accepted in last 30 day
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks for guest invites that were not redeemed within 30 days of the invitation.

Required permissions

  • User.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Guests are a feature of Entra ID that allows for business-to-business (B2B) collaboration with users external to your organization. Commonly used for Microsoft 365 and application sharing with partners, vendors, contractors, and other users who collaborate with the organization but are not employees, a guest user will be represented in the organizations Entra tenant. Guest users are not members of the organization, have a different risk profile, and are considered less trusted due to the organization not having full control over and insight into the user object and the devices they come from. Unused guest invitations can result in excessive permissions on resources, however, there are also legitimate reasons guests may exist in a tenant without redeeming an invite.

An attacker may target a guest account for compromise, and access the associated resources that the guest was assigned permission to.
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} Guest invitations that were not accepted within 30 days of the invitation
Indicator.Remediation:
Guest accounts that have not accepted their invitation should be evaluated for cleanup.

Guest users can server multiple purposes in an Entra tenant for an organization. If guest users have never redeemed their invitation, then the intended access to the resources they have been assigned permissions has never been used, and may result in excessive unnecessary permissions.

Guest users though may be created for legitimate reasons in an Entra tenant, including:

  • Membership in an Office 365 group
  • Membership in an Exchange Online distribution list
  • Membership in a Teams channel for email
  • Guest accounts being used as contact objects

Organizations must review the legitimacy of why the guest users exist in the tenant, and make a decision on how to clean up or keep the accounts.

Indicator.Name: Conditional Access policy with Continuous Access Evaluation disabled
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks for Conditional Access policies that have Continuous Access Evaluation disabled.

Required permissions

  • Policy.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Continuous Access Evaluation helps organizations respond quicker to condition changes on user objects, by providing certain policy and security event information from Entra ID to downstream applications. As an example, with CAE enabled, if a user account is disabled, Entra ID can notify Exchange Online and SharePoint Online of the event, causing the user to lose their existing session with those services. With CAE disabled, the applications rely on the lifetime of the access token before the user may lose access to these systems. CAE provides several benefits to both user and workload identities from a security perspective, and disabling will result in degraded fidelity to respond to events.

References

Continuous access evaluation in Microsoft Entra ID - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} Conditional Access policies with Continuous Access Evaluation disabled.
Indicator.Remediation:
Enable Continuous Access Evaluation on the indicated Conditional Access policies in Entra ID.

To enable, browse to the impacted policies in the Conditional Access blade, Conditional Access - Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link).

To browse to the settings in the Microsoft Entra admin center, expand the Protection menu on the left, select Conditional Access. On the right you will want to select Policies and find the impacted policies in the list. Select the policy, which will open the settings for it. Under Access controls select the Session setting. In the window that opens on the right, uncheck the box for Customize continuous access evaluation.

Indicator.Name: Users can create security groups
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks if non-admin users can create security groups.

Required permissions

  • Policy.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
By default all users are allowed to create security groups in an Entra tenant.

While many organizations may desire users to be able to create M365 groups for collaborations, valid cases for users creating security groups are generally limited.

Entra ID does not require unique group names, and an attacker may create malicious security groups with duplicate names of valid groups, in an effort to have the malicious group assigned further privileges.

References

Default user permissions - Microsoft Entra | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Any user can create security groups
Indicator.Remediation:
Disable Users can create security groups in the Entra tenant.

To disable, open the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link), expand Identity, expand Users, and select User settings.

In the blade on the right, toggle Users can create security groups to no.

Select Save at the bottom of the blade.

Organizations should understand how this change impacts their users and communicate the change out according to defined business processes.

Indicator.Name: Conditional Access policies contain private IP addresses
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks if any named locations contains private IP addresses and are assigned to enabled Conditional Access policies.

Required permissions

  • Policy.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Named locations should define public egress IP addresses or ranges, as Entra ID will see the public address of a user when evaluating Conditional Access policies.

If a private IP address or range is defined in a named location, it may result in the associated Conditional Access policies not operating as intended. Depending on how the policy is defined, it may present a security posture lower than intended.

The attack surface would be specific to the intention of the impacted Conditional Access policy.

References

Using networks and countries/regions in Microsoft Entra ID - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: There are Conditional Access policies referencing named locations containing private IP Addresses. These private addresses will not be considered while access is determined.
Indicator.Remediation:
Review the impacted Conditional Access policy, and verify the policy conditions are working as expected.

If the policy is working as expected, the Named location should be removed from the policy, as private IP addresses or ranges are not used in Conditional Access evaluation.

If the policy is not working as expected and is designed to evaluate based on network location, the Named location should be changed to reflect the egress IP addresses or ranges.

Organizations must ensure that all Conditional Access policy changes are documented and verify that they do not cause unintended negative impact

Named locations can be reviewed in the Entra admin center (direct link). To browse to them, on the left menu, expand Protection and then expand Conditional Access. In the blade on the right, select Named locations.

Indicator.Name: Conditional Access Policy that does not require MFA when sign-in risk has been identified
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks whether a Conditional Access policy exists that requires MFA if the authentication request risk is medium or high as determined by Entra ID Protection.

Required permissions

  • Policy.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Entra ID Protection uses several signals to determine the risk level when a user authenticates to Entra ID. This risk level on sign-in can be used to evaluate whether the authentication event occurring is genuine or malicious.

An attacker may compromise a user account in different ways, either by trying to authenticate as the user with a stolen password, or perform actions such as token replay. These attacks can generate a risk level in Entra ID Protection, that can be used in Conditional Access to force a request for MFA, which can prevent the user from leveraging the stolen credentials to access organizational resources.

References

What are risks in Microsoft Entra ID Protection - Microsoft Entra ID Protection | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: There are no Conditional Access Policies configured to require MFA if sign-in risk is detected to be medium or high.
Indicator.Remediation:
Deploy a Conditional Access policy requiring MFA for risky sign-ins.

Microsoft provides both a Conditional Access policy template, as well as step-by-step instructions for creating a Conditional Access Policy requiring MFA for risky sign-ins.

Implementing changes to Conditional Access policies can negatively impact users if not properly tested. Organizations need to verify all policy and security changes and communicate them out accordingly.

Indicator.Name: Objects with Reanimate-Tombstones extended right
Indicator.Description: This indicator checks if there are any non-default principals with the Reanimate-Tombstones extended right configured.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
When an Active Directory object is deleted, it is by default transformed into a tombstone object and loses most of its attributes. If the Active Directory Recycle Bin optional feature is enabled, the object is instead transformed into a deleted-object, which maintains the state of the object before deletion and can be undeleted without loss of information.

By default, only domain administrators can restore deleted objects. For other users, restoring these types of AD deleted objects requires having the "Reanimate-Tombstones" extended right. It is not recommended to delegate this right to other users since tombstone reanimation is a highly privileged and infrequent operation.

Furthermore, this permission could assist attackers with privilege escalation and lateral movement opportunities, as it could permit them to restore account objects with access to more resources. As tombstones don't maintain the state of the original object, reanimating them essentially grants control over the account because the user must set a password for the restored account.

References

Restoring Deleted Objects | Microsoft Learn

Creating and Deleting Objects in Active Directory Domain Services | Microsoft Learn

Recycle Bin Optional Feature | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} principal(s) with the "Reanimate-Tombstones" extended right.
Indicator.Remediation:
By default, only domain administrators can restore deleted objects, and it is not recommended to delegate the "Reanimate-Tombstones" right to other users since tombstone reanimation is a highly privileged and infrequent operation.

The "Reanimate Tombstone" control access right is granted at the Naming Context root. To remove the detected privilege assignments, perform the following steps:

  1. Open Active Directory Users and Computers (dsa.msc) and check the Advanced Features option under View.
  2. Right-click the domain root object and select Properties to view its properties.
  3. Click the Security tab and select the detected object in the "Group or user names" pane.
  4. Scroll down in the permissions pane to locate the "Reanimate tombstones" permission entry. If the permission isn't visible, you may need to find it using the Advanced button below the pane.
  5. Deselect the "Allow" checkbox for the permission, then click Apply to save the change.

References

Restoring Deleted Objects | Microsoft Learn

Indicator.Name: GPO Weak LM Hash storage enabled
Indicator.Description: This indicator detects when the "Network security: Do not store LAN Manager hash value on next password change" Group Policy Object setting is disabled within the Windows operating system.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
When the "Network security: Do not store LAN Manger hash value on next password change" setting is disabled, LAN Manager (LM) hashes continue to be stored during password changes. Enabling this setting strengthens security by preventing the storage of weak LM hashes and promoting the use of stronger password storage mechanisms. If an attacker gains access to the system or manages to extract the stored LM hashes, they can employ offline cracking techniques to obtain the original passwords. Therefore, monitoring and enabling this setting is crucial for mitigating the risk of unauthorized access and data breaches caused by the exploitation of weak LM hashes.

References

MS GPO information
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} Group Policies with Network security: Do not store LAN Manager hash value on next password change value disabled.
Indicator.Remediation:
Microsoft best practices for this value are:

  • Set Network security: Do not store LAN Manager hash value on next password change to Enabled.
  • Require all users to set new passwords the next time they sign in to the domain so that LAN Manager hashes are removed.

Indicator.Name: Writable shortcuts found in GPO
Indicator.Description: This indicator looks for shortcuts within Group Policy Objects (GPOs) that are writable by low privilege users. GPOs are a powerful feature in Windows domains that are used to manage various settings and configurations for multiple computers and users. Shortcuts are links to files or applications that can be deployed using GPOs. When low privilege users have the ability to modify these shortcuts, it could potentially lead to security risks and unauthorized modifications. This indicator helps organizations to identify such misconfigurations and take appropriate actions.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Changing a shortcut within a GPO, allows an attacker to perform the following:

  • Unauthorized Modifications - Low privilege users could make unauthorized changes to the files, compromising their integrity and potentially causing unintended behavior or security vulnerabilities.
  • Malicious Content Execution - If the files are replaced with malicious content, all users running them could unknowingly execute malicious code, leading to system compromise or unauthorized access to sensitive information.
  • System Instability - Unauthorized modifications to files can result in system instability, causing application errors, data corruption, or system crashes.
  • Compliance and Legal Consequences - If the affected files are critical for compliance or legal requirements, unauthorized modifications may lead to non-compliance, financial losses, reputational damage, or legal repercussions.
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} writable shortcuts in GPOs that could be modified by low privilege users.
Indicator.Remediation: It is crucial to review and adjust permissions on the identified writable shortcuts to ensure that only authorized users have the right access to them.

Indicator.Name: OU permissions enabling BadSuccessor dMSA escalation
Indicator.Description: This indicator checks for excessive permissions to create delegated Managed Service Accounts (dMSAs) within Active Directory organizational units (OU) or NC head (NC Head is the top-level object of a Naming Context (NC) in Active Directory) across domains that contain Windows Server 2025 domain controllers. The indicator identifies non-privileged identities that have been granted CreateChild, GenericAll, WriteDACL, or WriteOwner permissions on OUs or NC head, which could enable the execution of the "BadSuccessor" Privilege Escalation attack. The "BadSuccessor" attack is a technique which exploits vulnerabilities in Microsoft's Active Directory and enables domain takeover. The attack leverages a feature called Delegated Managed Service Accounts (dMSA), which was introduced in Windows Server 2025.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Excessive OU-level permissions, such as CreateChild on an OU or write rights on a dMSA object, can be abused to elevate privileges. In particular, the "BadSuccessor" attack simulates a completed dMSA migration to inherit a target account's security context. In environments with weak OU/NC head ACLs, the risk is moderate to high, although enhanced controls in Windows Server 2025 domains can reduce exposure.

References

BadSuccessor: Abusing dMSA to Escalate Privileges in Active Directory

KB5008383 — Active Directory permissions updates (CVE-2021-42291)

Setting up delegated Managed Service Accounts
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} OUs with excessive permissions.
Indicator.Remediation:
Limit the ability to create delegated Managed Service Accounts (dMSAs) by applying the principle of least privilege. Ensure only highly privileged and appropriately monitored administrative accounts should have permissions to create dMSAs across the domain. To modify OU or NC head permissions, open Active Directory Users and Computers (ADUC), right-click the relevant object, select "Security," and remove the vulnerable permissions from the affected users. Enumerate all non-default principals with dMSA creation permissions and identify the organizational units or NC head where these permissions are granted. Thoroughly review the output to determine if each identity requires this level of access and remove unnecessary permissions from the corresponding OU Access Control Lists (ACLs).

Before implementing any permission changes, test modifications in a controlled environment to ensure proper functionality of existing services and avoid unintended disruptions to domain operations.

Indicator.Name: Changes to unprivileged group membership in the last 7 days
Indicator.Description: This indicator shows unprivileged groups with membership changes made during the last seven days.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise: Membership changes to unprivileged groups may give access to resources using the group privileges.
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} unprivileged groups with membership changes.
Indicator.Remediation: Confirm that any additions/removals are valid from the suspicious groups

Indicator.Name: Application name and geographic location additional contexts are disabled on MFA
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks if the Microsoft Authenticator App settings are configured to display the name of the target application and geographical location of the request when a user receives a push notification.

Required permissions

  • Policy.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Push notification bombing is a tactic where an attacker will spam a user with excessive push notifications; users who are enabled for passwordless through the Microsoft Authenticator App can receive these with just knowing their username. While number matching in Entra ID primarily prevents users from accepting malicious push notifications, users can have better insight into the requests with additional context. When additional context is enabled, a user will see the geographical location from where the request came, as well as the name of the requesting application. Users can use this to verify that the request is legitimate.

References

Use additional context in Microsoft Authenticator notifications - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Application name and geographic location additional contexts on MFA are disabled.
Indicator.Remediation:
Enable Show application name in push and passwordless notifications and Show geographic location in push and passwordless notifications in Entra ID.

The configuration for additional context settings passwords can be found in Authentication methods - Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link).

To browse to the settings in the Microsoft Entra admin center, expand the Protection menu on the left, select Authentication methods, and then Policies. Once in Authentication methods, select Microsoft Authenticator on the right blade. The Microsoft Authenticator settings blade will open, and from the top menu select Configure. In the list of settings the status for Show application name in push and passwordless notifications and Show geographic location in push and passwordless notifications should both be changed to Enabled. The Target for the settings should be Include and set to All users.

Indicator.Name: Non-admin users can create tenants
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks if non-admin users are allowed to create Entra tenants.

Required permissions

  • Policy.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
By default all users are allowed to create additional Entra tenants from an existing Entra tenant. When created, the user will be invited into the new Entra tenant as a guest and will have Global Administrator access within that tenant. While the new Entra tenant will not have any resources associated with it, organizations may want to limit this from a shadow IT perspective.

If users start associating resources with this tenant, and it is not managed and monitored properly, it may become an easy target for an attacker.

References

Default user permissions - Microsoft Entra | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Non-admin users are able to create Entra tenants
Indicator.Remediation:
Enable Restrict non-admin users from creating tenants in the Entra tenant.

To enable, open the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link), expand Identity, expand Users, and select User settings.

In the blade on the right, toggle Restrict non-admin users from creating tenants to yes.

Select Save at the bottom of the blade.

Organizations should understand how this change impacts their users and communicate the change out according to defined business processes.

Indicator.Name: Users or devices inactive for at least 90 days
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks for users or devices that have not signed in to Entra ID in the past 90 days.

Required permissions

  • Device.Read.All
  • User.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
As part of an identity governance process and lifecycle, organizations should evaluate users and devices that have been inactive for 90 days or more, as it may be likely that the user or device is "stale" and no longer in use.

Inactive users many times still have access to all the corporate data and systems associated with their role. An attacker may target inactive users to compromise their account, and then exfiltrate data, perform reconnaissance, move laterally, and abuse the users mailbox for actions such as business email compromise. As inactive users are not regularly monitored by the business units associated with their job function, these actions may go unnoticed by the organization.

References

How to manage inactive user accounts - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn

How to manage stale devices in Microsoft Entra ID - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn

NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} users or devices that have been inactive for more than 90 days
Indicator.Remediation:
Evaluate the indicated inactive users and devices for management as per your organizational policy.

If the organization does not have a policy defining account management, NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 covers account lifecycle controls under AC-2 (3).

Indicated user objects can be found under the Entra admin center (direct link), expand Identity, expand Users, and select All users. You can search for the impacted users in the right blade.

Indicated user objects can be found under the Entra admin center (direct link), expand Identity, expand Devices, and select All devices. You can search for the impacted users in the right blade.

Indicator.Name: User consent is allowed for risky applications
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks for an Entra ID authorization policy that allows users to grant consent for risky applications.

Required permissions

  • Policy.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Risk-based step-up consent is a configuration setting in Entra ID that prevents users from consenting to delegated application permissions from applications that Entra ID may consider risky. When disabled, organizations are at a greater risk of users falling victim to OAuth consent phishing, also known as Illicit Consent Grants. Organizations should develop and implement a business process around the lifecycle of app registrations in their Entra tenant. Ensure that the added read permissions align with organizational policy. Organizations may want to consider using admin consent workflows as an additional defense against Illicit Consent Grants.

References

Configure risk-based step-up consent - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn

Update authorizationpolicy - Microsoft Graph beta | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: User consent for risky applications is allowed
Indicator.Remediation:
Enable risk-based step-up consent in Entra ID.

The configuration for enabling risk-based step-up consent is not exposed in the Entra admin center, and must be configured either through PowerShell or the Graph API.

Indicator.Name: Enterprise applications using SAML for SSO
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks for enterprise applications (service principals) in Entra ID configured to use SAML for SSO.

Required permissions

  • Application.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
When an enterprise application is configured for SAML authentication, either a Microsoft Entra generated self-signed certificate can be used for response signing, or alternatively, an externally generated certificate can be imported.

Applications using SAML can be prone to Silver SAML, a type of SAML response forging, if an attacker is able to obtain the signing certificate used by Entra ID. Externally generated signing certificates create an unnecessary attack surface for an attacker to be able to obtain the signing certificate. It is recommended that organizations use the generated self-signed certificates from Microsoft, as the private key cannot be exported from Entra ID, preventing the ability to perform a Silver SAML attack.

References

Meet Silver SAML: Golden SAML in the cloud (semperis.com)
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} service principals configured to use SAML.
Indicator.Remediation:
Verify that the certificate assigned to the application is a Microsoft generated self-signed certificate.

To review the certificate, open the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link), expand Identity, expand Applications, and select Enterprise applications.

In the blade on the right, search for the application, and select it.

In the new blade, under Manage select Single sign-on. Under SAML Certificates, you can select Download to download a copy of the current signing certificate, and then review the details of the certificate.

If the certificate is self-signed, it will contain CN = Microsoft Azure Federated SSO Certificate as the issuer and subject. Note, however, that an externally generated certificate could be produced with the same issuer and subject name, so organizations should be aware of any certificate rotations.

Organizations must coordinate certificate rotations with the application owner, as a certificate rotation must occur in both the Service Provider (SP) and Entra ID, or else authentication for the application will stop working.

Indicator.Name: MFA bombing attack occurred in the past day
Indicator.Description:
This indicator detects MFA bombing attempts on privileged accounts by monitoring login activity to identify unusual patterns of login activity of more than 5 failed MFA attempts within the last day. Note that this indicator will not detect activity of users using passwordless authentication.

Required permissions

  • AuditLog.Read.All
  • Directory.Read.All
  • RoleManagement.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise: MFA bombing is a tactic where an attacker spams the user with MFA requests and the user unknowingly or unwillingly accepts the request. MFA bombing attacks can be successful in certain cases, particularly if the system's MFA protections are not configured properly or are not robust enough to withstand a high volume of login attempts.
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} suspected MFA bombing attempts.
Indicator.Remediation:
To mitigate the risk of MFA bombing attacks, consider implementing the following measures:

  1. Reset the MFA method for the affected accounts.
  2. Set up MFA challenge policies that require users to complete additional authentication steps (e.g. answering security questions, entering a code sent to their phone) after a certain number of login failures.
  3. Increase the rate limiting on MFA attempts. This will make it harder for an attacker to brute force MFA codes.
  4. Use a hardware token for MFA. Hardware tokens are more secure than SMS or phone call-based MFA, as they cannot be easily intercepted or spoofed.
  5. Use a trusted IP range for MFA. If you have a specific range of IP addresses that you trust (e.g. your office location), you can configure MFA to only be required when logging in from outside of that range.
Note: It is important to carefully consider the potential impact of these changes on your users before implementing them. Some of these measures may be more effective at preventing MFA bombing attacks, but they may also be more inconvenient for your users.

Indicator.Name: Query policies that have the attribute of LDAP deny list set
Indicator.Description: This security indicator is designed to check for LDAP IP deny lists across multiple domains in an Active Directory environment. For each available domain, it query the Active Directory for "query policies" associated with that domain, specifically looking for LDAP IP deny lists (ldapipdenylist attribute).
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Unauthorized or unexpected entries in the LDAP IP deny list could suggest a security breach or an attempt to limit access to critical resources maliciously. The likelihood of compromise depends on the following factors:
  1. Unexpected Changes: Unauthorized modifications to the LDAP IP deny list.
  2. Unknown IP Addresses: Presence of IP addresses that are not recognized or authorized by the network administration team.
  3. Security Policy Violations: Entries that violate the organization's established security policies.
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} query policies with ldap deny list set.
Indicator.Remediation:
To mitigate potential security risks associated with the LDAP IP deny list, the following steps are recommended:
  1. Regular Audits: Conduct regular reviews of the LDAP IP deny list to ensure all entries are authorized and legitimate.
  2. Change Management: Implement strict change management procedures for modifications to the LDAP IP deny list. (Semperis DSP for example).
  3. Security Policy Violations: Entries that violate the organization's established security policies.
  4. Security Monitoring: Utilize security information and event management (SIEM) tools to monitor for unauthorized changes or access attempts blocked by the deny list.

Indicator.Name: List of Risky users (medium or high level)
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks for risky users in the tenant with medium or high level of risk.

Required permissions

  • IdentityRiskyUser.Read.All
  • Organization.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Risky users are individuals or accounts exhibiting behaviors increasing the likelihood of security incidents or breaches, such as weak authentication practices, susceptibility to phishing, unusual activity patterns, accessing resources from unsecured devices or networks, or holding elevated privileges. These users pose significant risks, potentially leading to credential compromises, data breaches, or insider threats.

Risky users pose a significant threat in enterprise identity (Entra ID) systems due to their potential to compromise sensitive data, systems, and resources. These users may exhibit behaviors such as using weak passwords, falling victim to phishing attacks, accessing resources from unsecured devices or networks, or exhibiting anomalous activity patterns. Their actions can lead to unauthorized access, data breaches, system disruptions, and other security incidents, posing substantial risks to the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of enterprise resources. Moreover, compromised user accounts can serve as footholds for attackers to escalate privileges, move laterally within the network, and carry out further malicious activities, amplifying the impact of security breaches.
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} risky users with high or medium risk.
Indicator.Remediation: Remediating risky users in enterprise identity (Entra ID) systems involves implementing a multi-faceted approach that includes comprehensive risk assessment, user education and training on security best practices, enforcement of strong authentication measures such as multi-factor authentication, regular review and adjustment of user access privileges to adhere to the principle of least privilege, continuous monitoring and anomaly detection for early identification of suspicious activities, establishment of an effective incident response plan for prompt containment and remediation of security incidents, and collaboration with security partners and regulatory bodies to stay abreast of emerging threats and best practices. By implementing these measures, organizations can mitigate the risks associated with risky users and enhance the overall security posture of their Entra ID systems.

Indicator.Name: More than 10 Privileged Administrators exist
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks for the presence of 10 or more Privileged Assigned Roles.

Required permissions

  • RoleManagement.Read.Directory
  • RoleManagement.Read.All
  • Directory.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Users that hold the a privileged assigned role are one of the most privileged users in Entra ID. Organizations should limit the number of privileged roles to less than ten.

An attacker will find users with the privileged role as high-value, and a compromised privileged role can lead to several attacks against the organization, that can include data exfiltration, lateral movement into other systems in Azure, multi-cloud, and on-premise systems, and can result in complete tenant compromise where an organization becomes locked out of their Entra tenant by the attacker.

References

Create an access review of Azure resource and Microsoft Entra roles in PIM - Microsoft Entra ID Governance | Microsoft Learn

Best practices for Microsoft Entra roles - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn

Manage emergency access admin accounts - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} privileged roles in Azure AD.
Indicator.Remediation:
Review the users assigned the selected privileged role, and determine if the role assignment can be removed.

To review and manage role assignments, open the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link), expand Identity, select Show more, expand Roles & admins, and select Roles & admins.

In the blade on the right, find the privileged roles role. Select each privileged role.

From this view you can remove users as necessary from the role. Organizations that have Entra ID P2 licensing available should use access reviews to manage the lifecycle of privileged users.

Organizations must ensure that any removal of the privileged role assignment does not impact the ability to manage their Entra tenant.

Indicator.Name: Privileged accounts with mailbox
Indicator.Description:
This indicator searches for privileged accounts in the Entra tenant that have an associated mailbox.

Required permissions

  • RoleManagement.Read.All
  • RoleManagement.Read.Directory
  • Directory.Read.All
  • MailboxSettings.Read
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Privileged accounts in the tenant that have a mailbox may be a prime target to attackers. Attackers may try to use the mailbox address of privileged users to achieve tokens to gain access, obtain sensitive data, or move laterally through the environment.

References

Enable per-user Microsoft Entra multifactor authentication to secure sign-in events
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} privileged Entra ID accounts that have mailboxes.
Indicator.Remediation:
Monitor the detected privileged users' mailboxes to ensure that they are not being used in a non-administrative way. In addition, use unified audit logs to investigate suspicious activity. Also regularly rotate passwords and avoid using default or easily guessable passwords. Furthermore, enforce strong password policies for administrative accounts and train administrators on security best practices, including identifying phishing attempts and avoiding suspicious links. Another important thing is to enable MFA for privileged accounts, to add an extra layer of security by requiring a second form of authentication.

To enable MFA for a user:

Sign in to the Microsoft Entra admin center as at least an Authentication Administrator. Browse to Identity, select users and then All users. Click on Per-user MFA. Find the privileged user you want to enable for per-user Microsoft Entra multifactor authentication. You might need to change the view at the top to users. Then Check the box next to the name(s) of the user(s) to change the state for. On the right-hand side, under quick steps and choose Enable.

Indicator.Name: Less than 2 Global Administrators exist
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks for the presence of less than 2 Global Administrators.

Required permissions

  • RoleManagement.Read.Directory
  • RoleManagement.Read.All
  • Directory.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Users that hold the Global Administrator role are the most privileged users in Entra ID. According to Microsoft's recommendations, organizations should have at least 2 Global Administrators.

Having at least 2 Global Administrators ensures continuous access management and prevents single points of failure in administrative tasks.

References

Create an access review of Azure resource and Microsoft Entra roles in PIM - Microsoft Entra ID Governance | Microsoft Learn

Secure your organization's identities with Microsoft Entra ID - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn

Manage emergency access admin accounts - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} Global Administrators in Entra ID.
Indicator.Remediation:
Create more than one Global Administrator.

To review and manage role assignments, open the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link), expand Identity, select Show more, expand Roles & admins, and select Roles & admins.

In the blade on the right, find the Global Administrator role. Select the role.

From this view you can add/remove users as necessary from the role. Organizations that have Entra ID P2 licensing available should use access reviews to manage the lifecycle of privileged users.

Organizations must ensure that any addition/removal of the Global Administrator role does not impact the ability to manage their Entra tenant.

Indicator.Name: Application instance property lock disabled
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks for applications that have app instance property lock disabled.

Required permissions

  • Application.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Application instance property lock is a feature in Entra ID that restricts the ability to add or modify sensitive properties on service principals.

Attackers can look for privileged service principals, and attempt to use them to move laterally, including by adding credentials onto the existing service principal, if they gain access to a user with the ability to do so. App instance property lock can prevent this.

References

How to configure app instance property lock in your applications - Microsoft identity platform | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} applications that have a sensitive property lock disabled.
Indicator.Remediation:
Enable the application instance lock feature and all of its properties for app registrations.

To enable the instance property lock of an application, Sign in to Microsoft Entra admin center as at least a Cloud Application Administrator. In the Applications tab, click on App Registrations and select the application to be configured. Select Authentication, and then select Configure under the App instance property lock section. In the App instance property lock pane, enter the settings for the lock.

Indicator.Name: Unprivileged owner of a privileged group
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks for the presence of an unprivileged owner of a group with privileged roles.

Required permissions

  • RoleManagement.Read.Directory
  • Directory.Read.All
  • GroupMember.Read.All
  • RoleAssignmentSchedule.Read.Directory
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Assigning roles to a group instead of individual principals streamlines the process of adding or removing users and ensures consistent permissions for all group members. However, a group owner can add themselves to the group at any time, posing a privilege escalation risk if the owner is an unprivileged principal. Therefore, organizations should monitor group ownership closely to ensure it is not assigned to an unprivileged principal.

An attacker compromising an unprivileged group owner's account can exploit this to achieve privilege escalation by adding their compromised account (or any other account) to the privileged group and inherit all the permissions associated with that group.

References

Create an access review of Azure resource and Microsoft Entra roles in PIM - Microsoft Entra ID Governance | Microsoft Learn

Use Microsoft Entra groups to manage role assignments - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} unprivileged owners of a privileged groups in Entra ID
Indicator.Remediation:
Review the principals assigned the ownership of a given privileged group, and determine if the ownership can be removed.

To review and manage role assignments, open the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link), expand Privileged Identity Management, select the privileged group name, expand Roles under the Manage blade, and select Owner.

Select each unprivileged principal and consider its ownership.

From this view you can remove principals as necessary from the group's ownership.

Organizations must ensure that any removal of the unprivileged owners does not impact the ability to manage their Entra tenant.

Indicator.Name: Prohibited Entra ID Roles Assigned
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks for the assignment of roles in Entra ID that are deprecated or prohibited from use.

Required permissions

  • RoleManagement.Read.Directory
  • RoleManagement.Read.All
  • Directory.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Deprecated roles and roles marked as "never use" can pose significant security risks if assigned. These roles may have unintended permissions or may not be properly maintained, potentially leading to security vulnerabilities.

An attacker who gains access to an account with these roles could potentially exploit outdated or overly broad permissions, leading to unauthorized access, data breaches, or further privilege escalation within the Entra ID environment.

References

Best practices for Microsoft Entra roles - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} principals with prohibited roles assigned. {1} 'Deprecated' roles and {2} 'Prohibited' roles detected.
Indicator.Remediation:
Review all principals with prohibited role assignments and remove these assignments immediately.

To review and manage role assignments, open the Microsoft Entra admin center, go to Identity > Roles and admins.

For each prohibited role:

  1. Identify alternative, supported roles that provide the necessary permissions.
  2. Remove the prohibited role assignment.
  3. Assign the appropriate alternative role.
  4. Verify that the principal can still perform their required tasks with the new role assignment.

For deprecated roles, consult Microsoft documentation to find the recommended replacement roles.

For "prohibited" roles, revoke all assignments and reassess the need for those permissions.

Caution: Ensure that removing these role assignments does not disrupt critical operations. Always test changes in a non-production environment first.

Indicator.Name: Self-service password reset enabled for privileged roles
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks whether users in privileged roles in Entra ID can use self-service password reset.

Required permissions

  • Policy.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Self-service password reset (SSPR) is beneficial in organizations for end users but has security trade-offs for privileged accounts. For details on the full list of privileged roles impacted by this, see the reference article.

Attackers can and will target privileged users for compromise. Not all methods available for SSPR are phishing-resistant, and an attacker can leverage this to gain access to a privileged users account.

References

Self-service password reset policies - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn

authorizationPolicy resource type - Microsoft Graph v1.0 | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: SSPR is enabled for privileged roles.
Indicator.Remediation:
Disable SSPR for privileged roles.

To disable SSPR for privileged roles, you cannot use the Entra admin center. Organizations must use either the Microsoft Graph API or the Microsoft Graph PowerShell SDK to disable SSPR for privileged roles. See the reference articles for details on how to disable. The policy setting AllowedToUseSSPR should be set to false.

Organizations must ensure that disabling SSPR for privileged roles does not negatively impact their ability to administer their Entra tenant, and should ensure their break-glass accounts are working and maintained prior to disabling.

Indicator.Name: Ensure all non-privileged users can complete MFA
Indicator.Description:
Indicates whether non-privileged users in the tenant can use and complete the MFA process based on the authentication details that the user entered.

Required permissions

  • AuditLog.Read.All
  • User.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
MFA adds an extra layer of security to the sign-in process, helping protect your organization against security breaches. Without MFA, relying solely on one authentication factor makes accounts more susceptible to unauthorized access. If an attacker obtains a user's password (through phishing, data breaches, or other means), they can easily gain access to the account. In addition, users who use MFA are less likely to fall victim to phishing attacks. MFA helps mitigate this risk because even if a user unknowingly provides their password, the attacker still needs the second factor (e.g., a code from an authenticator app) to gain access.

References

Security defaults in Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn

Require multifactor authentication for all users | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} users that cannot complete the MFA process with strong methods.
Indicator.Remediation:
Enforce MFA authentication for users in the tenant by Conditional Access Policies. In case no Premium P1/P2 licenses are used, enable Security Defaults in Entra ID.

To Create MFA access condition policy:

Browse to Protection, Conditional Access and then Policies. Select New policy. Give your policy a name. Under Assignments, select Users or workload identities. Under Include, select All users. Under Exclude select Users and groups and choose your organization's emergency access or break-glass accounts. Under Target resources, Resources (formerly cloud apps) and under Include, select All resources (formerly 'All cloud apps'). Under Exclude, select any applications that don't require multifactor authentication. Under Access controls, click on Grant, select Grant access. Select "Require authentication strength", then select the built-in "Multifactor authentication strength" from the list. Select Select. Select Create to create to enable your policy.

To enable Security Defaults:

Browse to Identity and then Overview and Properties. Select Manage security defaults. Set Security defaults to Enabled. Select Save.

Indicator.Name: Permanent Active Privileged Role Assignment
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks for the presence of permanent active privileged role Assignments.

Required permissions

  • RoleManagement.Read.Directory
  • Directory.Read.All
  • Application.Read.All
  • GroupMember.Read.All
  • User.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Privileged roles in Microsoft Entra Privileged Identity Management (PIM) that are assigned as "permanently active" pose significant security risks. According to Microsoft Learn, "Privileged role administrators can make users active for Microsoft Entra admin roles. An active administrator can activate the role when they need it, and then their permissions expire once they're done." However, allowing principals to have permanent active for privileged roles is not the most secure practice, as it provides continuous access to elevated permissions.

If an administrator intends to grant a user the ability to perform privileged actions, it is advisable to provide a limited time window for activity rather than indefinite access. Longer periods of activity increase the risk of privilege escalation and potential misuse.

The likelihood of compromise increases significantly when privileged roles in PIM are assigned as permanently active. This configuration allows users continuous access to elevated permissions, increasing the risk of privilege escalation and potential exploitation.

References

Assign Microsoft Entra roles in Privileged Identity Management | Microsoft Learn

Assign Azure resource roles in Privileged Identity Management | Microsoft Learn

CISA MS.AAD.7.4v1
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} Permanent Active Privileged Role Assignments in Azure AD
Indicator.Remediation:
Limit the duration of activity for privileged roles in PIM to a reasonable timeframe (e.g., up to 90 days) based on the user's role and necessity.

To configure activity durations, open the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link), expand Identity, select, expand Identity governance, and select Privileged Identity Managemnet.

In the blade on the right, select the specific privileged role you wish to configure. Adjust the activity settings to ensure they are time-bound.

Conduct regular reviews of all privileged roles to ensure that only necessary users have active access, and that this access is appropriately time-bound.

Educate administrators and users about the risks of permanently active roles and the importance of maintaining strict access controls.

Indicator.Name: Entra tenant is susceptible to Hidden Consent Grant attack
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks app permissions and settings to determine if the Entra tenant is susceptible to Hidden Consent Grant attacks.

Required permissions

  • Directory.Read.All
  • Policy.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
A Hidden Consent Grant attack is a type of phishing attack where a malicious actor who controls an application with Directory.ReadWrite.All permissions gains access to an application and exploits granted permissions to escalate his privileges.

The Entra tenant contains applications with different permission requirements. That is, some applications require more permissive permissions, but other applications may have misconfigured permissions or are using default permissions that may not be required. If a malicious actor gets control of an application that allows them to create an app with a redirect to a URL pointing to a controlled endpoint (a pre-requirement of the attack), they may escalate their privileges within the Entra tenant.

References

A New App Consent Attack: Hidden Consent Grant | Semperis blog

Delegate application management administrator permissions - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn

Default user permissions - Microsoft Entra | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} scenarios of IOE that may lead to hidden consent grant attack.
Indicator.Remediation:
Review apps with AppRoleAssignment.ReadWrite.All, DelegatedPermissionGrant.ReadWrite.All or Directory.ReadWrite.All application permissions and remove any unnecessary app privileges. Monitor and revoke any unauthorized OAuth consent grants.

In addition, consider disabling the Users can register applications setting in the Entra tenant, which is enabled by default.

To disable this settings:

Open the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link), expand Identity, expand Users, and select User settings.

In the blade on the right, toggle Users can register applications to no.

Select Save at the bottom of the blade.

Organizations should understand how this change impacts their users and communicate the change out according to defined business processes.

Indicator.Name: Entra custom Roles with risky permissions
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks if a custom role contains risky permissions

Required permissions

  • RoleManagement.Read.Directory
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Custom roles with risky permissions can be abused by attackers in various ways depending on the specific permission that was granted. Potentially, custom roles can be used to gain access to sensitive information or perform malicious actions. More details on this attack can be found in the reference Semperis article.

References

Custom Roles Permissions - Semperis
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} custom roles with risky permissions.
Indicator.Remediation:
Review the permissions assigned to the custom role.

Organizations should determine if risky permissions cna be removed from the role. If not, use of the role should be limited as needed.

To review and manage role assignments, open the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link), expand Identity, select Show more, expand Roles & admins, and select Roles & admins.

In the blade on the right, find the custom role. Select the role. A new blade will open with the role settings. Under Manage in this blade, select Description.

From this view you can review role permissions. To remove permissions, select Edit.

Indicator.Name: Detect MFA policy changes for groups and users
Indicator.Description:
This indicator looks for changes (removal of users and groups) from MFA policies. It relates to events invoked more than 4 hours after the policy's creation.

Required permissions

  • AuditLog.Read.All
  • Policy.Read.All
  • GroupMember.Read.All
  • Reports.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
MFA policies are considered as a sensitive asset in the identity security of the organization, in cases of changes in membership of users and groups in MFA policies, it may indicate on compromise of users (in case it is a malicious action).

References

What are Microsoft Entra audit logs?
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} users/groups involved in MFA policy membership changes.
Indicator.Remediation: Review the changes in the MFA policy in the audit log (with the Category of Policy and activity of "Update policy") and make sure they were made by a valid user.

Indicator.Name: Password hash synchronization is not enabled
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks whether password hash synchronization is enabled in the tenant.

Required permissions

    OnPremDirectorySynchronization.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Password hash synchronization (PHS) is a feature of Entra ID that allows for authentication resilience over other methods of authentication, such as pass-through authentication (PTA) or federated authentication.

Organizations that must use PTA or federated authentication can still enable PHS to take advantage of features in Entra Identity Protection to check for leaked credentials. Further, PHS can be used as a fallback in PTA or federated authentication outage scenarios, even though the change from PTA or federated authentication to PHS is not immediate.

References

Password Hash Synchronization is automatically enabled in Microsoft Entra connector | Microsoft Learn

Implement password hash synchronization with Microsoft Entra Connect Sync - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} tenants with password hash synchronization disabled.
Indicator.Remediation:
Enable password hash synchronization in the Entra tenant.

When dealing with password hash synchronization, organizations have options to migrate from pass-through authentication (PTA) or federated authentication, or alternatively organizations can enable password hash synchronization and still leave the authentication method of PTA or federated in place.

Organizations enabling password hash synchronization must ensure they are not changing the authentication method without proper planning and testing.

Depending on whether the organization is looking to migrate from PTA or federated authentication, or if they are looking to enable password hash synchronization without changing authentication, the means of making this change are different in Entra connect.

Indicator.Name: Certificate-Based Authentication Persistence
Indicator.Description:
This indicator looks for the presence of specific Entra ID Microsoft Graph app roles and permissions that, when combined, can enable a user to establish persistence through certificate-based authentication (CBA).

Required permissions

  • RoleManagement.Read.Directory
  • RoleManagement.Read.All
  • Directory.Read.All
  • Application.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise: If an attacker is able to gain access to an Account with Authentication Policy Administrator role or an application with a combination of Policy.ReadWrite.AuthenticationMethod and Organization.ReadWrite.All permissions, they can log into Entra ID as any user, even a Global Administrator.
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} principals with at least one of the required permissions to be able to persist using CBA.
Indicator.Remediation:
To mitigate the risk associated with Certificate-based authentication (CBA) persistence, it is recommended that the organization review and evaluate the necessity of the permissions and role assignments that can be leveraged for a CBA attack.

Limit the assignment of the following permissions only to trusted apps that need the ability to add a Root CA if CBA is enabled:

  • Authentication Policy Administrator
  • Application with Policy.ReadWrite.AuthenticationMethod and Organization.ReadWrite.All permissions

Remove the following roles and permissions from any account that does not require them for operational purposes:

  • Privileged Role Administrator
  • Privileged Authentication Administrator
  • RoleManagement.ReadWrite.Directory
  • AppRoleAssignment.ReadWrite.All

Indicator.Name: User accounts using Smart Card authentication with old password
Indicator.Description: This indicator looks for user accounts that are using Smart Card authentication whose password has not changed in the last 90 days.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
A user may use a Smart Card for authentication instead of a password. By default, accounts with the "Smart Card required" flag enabled have disabled password rotation, which may weaken your organization's password rotation practices. This static password hash is used for signing Kerberos tickets and if compromised may lead to credential-theft based attacks including Silver Ticket attacks.

References

Set-ADDomain
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} users using Smart Card authentication whose password has not changed recently.
Indicator.Remediation:
Implement regular password changes for users. Additionally, for Windows 2016 or later domains, leverage the msDS-ExpirePasswordsOnSmartCardOnlyAccounts attribute to enforce periodic password hashing even for smart card users. This enhances security by mitigating the risk associated with static password hashes.

To enable this attribute, use the following command (to enable the attribute msDS-ExpirePasswordsOnSmartCardOnlyAccounts):

Set-ADDomain -Identity DomainName -PublicKeyRequiredPasswordRolling $True

Indicator.Name: Unresolved Entra ID privileged role members
Indicator.Description:
This indicator identifies privileged role assignments where the member cannot be found, either due to deleted eligible users or GDAP partner relationships.

Required permissions

  • RoleManagement.Read.All
  • Directory.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
When privileged role members cannot be found, it can indicate either deleted eligible users or hidden GDAP partner relationships. Deleted eligible users that remain in role assignments can create confusion and complicate role management. GDAP partner relationships, while legitimate, need to be properly tracked and managed to maintain security oversight.

Regular auditing of these situations is important to maintain clear visibility of who has access to privileged roles in your environment.
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} privileged role assignments where the member could not be found.
Indicator.Remediation:
To address ununresolved privileged role members, follow these steps based on the scenario:

For Deleted Users:

  1. Navigate to the Microsoft Entra admin center
  2. Use the provided UserPrincipalName and DisplayName to search in the Users blade
  3. If confirmed as deleted, remove their role eligibility through PIM

For GDAP Partner Relationships:

  1. Access the Microsoft Admin Center (direct link)
  2. Review all partner relationships and their associated role assignments
  3. Verify that each partner relationship is legitimate and necessary

Note: GDAP relationships cannot be viewed through Graph API from the managed tenant, but are visible in the admin portal to Global Administrators

Regular review of both scenarios is recommended as part of your security governance process.

Indicator.Name: Users are not using their privileged roles
Indicator.Description:
This indicator checks for users who have not activated their a role assigned to them for over 30 days.

For users eligible via group membership, audit log retention in Entra ID may prevent gathering an accurate 'EligibleSince' date.

Required permissions

  • RoleEligibilitySchedule.Read.Directory
  • GroupMember.Read.All
  • Directory.Read.All
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
Users holding excessive privileges that are not used expands an organizations attack surface area. Attackers can use any privileges assigned to users for attacks, including lateral movement, privileges escalation, implementing persistence, data exfiltration, and others with a compromised user that has privileges assigned.

Access Reviews can be used in Entra Privileged Identity Management (PIM) to manage the lifecycle fo assigned privileges for users.

References

Create an access review of Azure resource and Microsoft Entra roles in PIM - Microsoft Entra ID Governance | Microsoft Learn

NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} users eligible for highly privileged roles that have not activated their role in the past 30 days.
Indicator.Remediation:
Review the users identified and the unused roles they hold, and determine if they should continue to hold these unused roles. A privileged access strategy should follow a governance plan for assigning least privilege, such as that documented in NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5.

You can review existing role assignments in the Microsoft Entra admin center (direct link).

Organizations should use the Access Reviews feature of Entra Privileged Identity Management to help manage the lifecycle of privileged role assignments.

Indicator.Name: New permission has been granted to a group
Indicator.Description: In the last 7 days, permission was given to a group.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise: Members of a group with high privileges can perform significant actions in Okta, it is important to know which groups grant strong privileges.
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} events granting permissions to groups in the last week.
Indicator.Remediation: Make sure that the permissions given to the group are indeed necessary and were done legitimately.

Indicator.Name: New permission has been granted to a user
Indicator.Description: This indicator checks if any permissions have been granted to a user within the last 7 days.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise: Users with high privileges can perform significant actions in Okta. It is important to identify and monitor users who have been granted elevated privileges to mitigate the risk of unauthorized access and potential misuse of sensitive data.
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} events granting permissions to users in the last week.
Indicator.Remediation: Regularly review and validate the permissions assigned to users. Ensure that the permissions granted are necessary for their roles and responsibilities. Remove any unnecessary or excessive permissions to minimize the attack surface and maintain least privilege access control.

Indicator.Name: Dangerous user rights granted by GPO
Indicator.Description: Group Policy Objects (GPOs) are used to define security settings that apply to a group of users or computers in an Active Directory environment. GPOs can be used to grant dangerous user rights, such as the ability to bypass file system security, log on as a service, or even perform actions with elevated privileges. This indicator looks for non-privileged users who are granted elevated permissions through GPO.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise:
An attacker can potentially exploit the user rights granted by a GPO to gain access to systems, steal sensitive information, or cause other types of damage. If these dangerous user rights are granted to a user or a group of users, it increases the risk of an attacker being able to gain access to sensitive data, systems or even perform malicious actions.

References

MS User Rights Assignment
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} non-privileged users with elevated permissions granted using GPO.
Indicator.Remediation: The remediation for this indicator involves identifying any dangerous user rights that have been granted through GPOs, and removing them wherever possible. This can involve reviewing existing GPO settings and modifying them to remove any unnecessary or excessive user rights, as well as implementing appropriate access controls to restrict access to GPOs and other critical Active Directory components. By taking these steps, organizations can reduce the risk of compromise and help protect their systems from potential attacks.

Indicator.Name: New API token was created
Indicator.Description: In the last 7 days,a new API token was created.
Indicator.LikelihoodOfCompromise: API tokens with high privileges can allow unauthorized access and actions in Okta. If an attacker gains access to the token's password, they can leverage it to query and perform actions, potentially leading to persistence and compromising the environment.
Indicator.ResultMessage: Found {0} new API tokens in the last week.
Indicator.Remediation: Ensure that the API token was created and used legitimately. Regularly review and audit API tokens to verify their necessity and permissions. Implement proper access controls and token management practices to mitigate the risk of unauthorized access.

Indicator.Name: Dangerous GPO logon script path
Indicator.Description: This indicator searches for logon script paths where the script does not exist and where a low-privilege user has permissions on the parent folder. Additionally, it checks for logon script paths where the script exists but low-privilege users have permissions to modify them.
